question: Which of the following is a legally binding offer under English contract law?

options.text: A shop displaying a bottle of wine with a £12 price tag
options.feedback: A shop display is an invitation to treat — _Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v Boots Cash Chemists (Southern) Ltd_ [1953] 1 QB 401.

options.text: A newspaper advertisement stating 'Black leather sofa £200 — first come first served'
options.feedback: Advertisements are generally invitations to treat — _Partridge v Crittenden_ [1968] 1 WLR 1204.

options.text: A website stating 'Items in your basket are subject to acceptance at checkout'
options.feedback: This describes an invitation to treat. The contract is formed at checkout.

options.text: A company's written statement: 'We will pay £100 to any person who uses our product as directed and still catches influenza'
options.feedback: _Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co_ [1893] 1 QB 256 established that advertisements can be offers when there is a clear promise to pay, demonstrated by the company depositing £1,000 in a bank as proof of sincerity.

oscoa_references: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256
Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v Boots Cash Chemists (Southern) Ltd [1953] 1 QB 401
Partridge v Crittenden [1968] 1 WLR 1204

question: 'A customer takes an item from a supermarket shelf and walks with it to the checkout. At this point, the customer has made an offer to purchase the item.' — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: The display of goods is an invitation to treat, not an offer. The customer makes the offer when presenting goods at the checkout — _Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v Boots Cash Chemists (Southern) Ltd_ [1953] 1 QB 401.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. _Boots_ [1953] 1 QB 401 established that goods on display are an invitation to treat. The customer offers to buy when presenting the goods at the checkout, and the cashier accepts.

oscoa_references: Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v Boots Cash Chemists (Southern) Ltd [1953] 1 QB 401

question: In _Fisher v Bell_ [1961] 1 QB 394, a shopkeeper displayed a flick knife in the shop window with a price tag. The court held:

options.text: The shopkeeper had made an offer to sell the knife
options.feedback: The court held the opposite — the display was not an offer.

options.text: The display was an invitation to treat, not an offer for sale
options.feedback: Correct. The display in the window was an invitation to treat. The shopkeeper could not be convicted of 'offering for sale' a prohibited weapon under the Restriction of Offensive Weapons Act 1959.

options.text: The shopkeeper committed an offence by advertising a prohibited weapon
options.feedback: The court acquitted the shopkeeper precisely because the display was not an offer.

options.text: The display constituted both an offer and an invitation to treat
options.feedback: These are mutually exclusive categories in English contract law — an offer cannot simultaneously be an invitation to treat.

oscoa_references: Fisher v Bell [1961] 1 QB 394
Restriction of Offensive Weapons Act 1959

question: A company publishes an advertisement: '£500 reward to anyone who catches the Lock Ness Monster using our fishing rod.' The company has £100,000 in its bank account. Is this an offer?

options.text: No — it is a puff or exaggeration
options.feedback: While the subject matter is fantastical, the offer could still be interpreted as serious if the company demonstrated sincerity of intent, as in _Carlill_ [1893].

options.text: Yes — it is a unilateral offer akin to _Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co_ [1893]
options.feedback: Correct. Following _Carlill_ [1893] 1 QB 256, where the company deposits money as proof of sincerity, an advertisement can constitute a unilateral offer capable of acceptance by performance.

options.text: No — advertisements are always invitations to treat
options.feedback: _Carlill_ [1893] is the leading exception — advertisements can be offers where there is proof of sincerity and intention to be bound.

options.text: Only if the reward is claimed within a reasonable time
options.feedback: Timing affects whether the offer has lapsed, not whether it is an offer in the first instance.

oscoa_references: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256

question: Under Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.57(2), a sale by auction is complete when the auctioneer announces its completion by the fall of the _______.
feedback: The correct answer is 'hammer'. Under s.57(2), each bid is an offer which is accepted by the fall of the hammer. Until that moment, any bid may be retracted.
oscoa_references: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.57(2)

question: An auction is advertised as 'without reserve'. The auctioneer refuses to accept the highest genuine bid. What is the legal position?

options.text: The highest bidder has no remedy — an auctioneer may refuse any bid
options.feedback: This is true for auctions with a reserve. Without reserve, different rules apply.

options.text: The auctioneer is in breach of a collateral contract with the highest bidder — _Barry v Davies_ [2000] 1 WLR 1962
options.feedback: Correct. _Barry v Davies_ [2000] held that an auction without reserve creates a collateral contract between the auctioneer and the highest bidder. The auctioneer is obliged to sell to the highest bidder.

options.text: The highest bidder becomes the owner of the goods by operation of law
options.feedback: No automatic transfer of ownership occurs — the remedy is damages for breach of the collateral contract.

options.text: The auction is void for uncertainty
options.feedback: The auction itself is valid. The relevant question is whether the auctioneer breached a contractual obligation.

oscoa_references: Barry v Davies [2000] 1 WLR 1962
Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.57

question: A company issues a circular stating: 'We invite tenders for the purchase of our stock.' In _Spencer v Harding_ (1870) LR 5 CP 561, the court held that such a circular is:

options.text: An offer to sell to the highest tender
options.feedback: The court specifically rejected this interpretation.

options.text: An invitation to treat — the company is not obligated to accept any tender
options.feedback: Correct. _Spencer v Harding_ (1870) established that a circular inviting tenders is an invitation to treat. The tenders themselves are offers, which the invitor may accept or reject.

options.text: A binding promise to sell to all tenderers
options.feedback: No — this would create a commercial impossibility.

options.text: A unilateral contract with each person who submits a tender
options.feedback: The circular alone does not create any binding obligation; the invitor retains discretion.

oscoa_references: Spencer v Harding (1870) LR 5 CP 561

question: A local council invites tenders for a concession to operate pleasure flights, stating 'The council will consider all conforming tenders.' Aero Club submits a valid tender, but the council accepts a late tender instead. Aero Club sues. Applying _Blackpool and Fylde Aero Club Ltd v Blackpool Borough Council_ [1990] 1 WLR 1195, what is the outcome?

options.text: Aero Club loses — the invitation to tender is merely an invitation to treat
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal held that an express promise to consider conforming tenders creates a contractual obligation.

options.text: Aero Club wins — the council breached an implied contractual obligation to consider the conforming tender
options.feedback: Correct. _Blackpool Aero Club_ [1990] established that where an invitation to tender expressly states that conforming tenders will be considered, a collateral contract arises. The council was obliged to consider and did not.

options.text: Aero Club wins — the late tender was automatically invalid
options.feedback: While the late tender was defective, the key issue was the council's failure to consider Aero Club's conforming tender, not the validity of the late one.

options.text: Aero Club loses — no contract existed until the council accepted a tender
options.feedback: A collateral contract existed separate from the main contract. The council's promise to 'consider' was binding.

oscoa_references: Blackpool and Fylde Aero Club Ltd v Blackpool Borough Council [1990] 1 WLR 1195
Spencer v Harding (1870) LR 5 CP 561

question: Which of the following events can terminate an offer? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Revocation communicated to the offeree before acceptance
options.feedback: Correct. _Routledge v Grant_ (1828) 4 Bing 653 — an offer may be revoked at any time before acceptance.

options.text: Rejection or counter-offer by the offeree
options.feedback: Correct. _Hyde v Wrench_ (1840) 3 Beav 334 — a counter-offer destroys the original offer.

options.text: Lapse of a reasonable period of time
options.feedback: Correct. _Ramsgate Victoria Hotel Co v Montefiore_ (1866) LR 1 Ex 109 — offers lapse after a reasonable time.

options.text: Death of the offeror where the offeree has notice
options.feedback: Correct. _Bradbury v Morgan_ (1862) 1 H&C 249 — death terminates an offer once the offeree has notice.

options.text: A mere change of mind by the offeror without communication
options.feedback: A change of mind alone is insufficient. Revocation must be communicated — _Dickinson v Dodds_ (1876) 2 Ch D 463.

oscoa_references: Routledge v Grant (1828) 4 Bing 653
Hyde v Wrench (1840) 3 Beav 334
Ramsgate Victoria Hotel Co v Montefiore (1866) LR 1 Ex 109
Bradbury v Morgan (1862) 1 H&C 249
Dickinson v Dodds (1876) 2 Ch D 463

question: Dodds offers to sell his house to Dickinson, saying 'This offer will be open until Friday 9am.' On Thursday, Dickinson learns from a reliable mutual friend that Dodds has sold the property to another buyer. On Friday at 8am, Dickinson delivers a letter of acceptance to Dodds. Is there a contract?

options.text: Yes — the offer was expressed to be open until Friday 9am
options.feedback: An offer expressed to be open for a period can still be revoked before acceptance, unless supported by consideration (making it an option).

options.text: No — the offer was validly revoked by reliable third-party communication — _Dickinson v Dodds_ (1876) 2 Ch D 463
options.feedback: Correct. _Dickinson v Dodds_ established that revocation need not be communicated directly — reliable third-party information suffices. Dickinson knew of the sale before accepting.

options.text: Yes — revocation must be communicated directly by the offeror
options.feedback: _Dickinson v Dodds_ specifically held that indirect communication via a reliable source is sufficient.

options.text: No — the promise to keep the offer open was not supported by consideration
options.feedback: While it's true that the promise lacked consideration (and thus was not an option), the key issue in _Dickinson v Dodds_ was the mode of revocation.

oscoa_references: Dickinson v Dodds (1876) 2 Ch D 463

question: A offers to sell his farm to B for £1,000. B replies: 'I will give you £950 for it.' A refuses. B then writes: 'I accept your original offer of £1,000.' A does not respond. Is there a contract?

options.text: Yes — B ultimately matched the original offer
options.feedback: The counter-offer destroyed the original offer. B cannot revive it — _Hyde v Wrench_ (1840) 3 Beav 334.

options.text: No — B's counter-offer of £950 destroyed the original offer; B's attempt to 'accept' £1,000 was a new offer which A rejected
options.feedback: Correct. _Hyde v Wrench_ (1840) established the mirror-image rule: a counter-offer acts as a rejection. The original offer is destroyed and cannot later be accepted. B's 'acceptance' was a fresh offer that A was free to reject.

options.text: Yes — the original offer remained open because A did not expressly withdraw it
options.feedback: Express withdrawal is not required — the counter-offer itself terminates the original offer automatically.

options.text: No — B's offer of £950 was too far below the asking price to be an effective counter-offer
options.feedback: Any counter-offer, regardless of proximity to the original terms, destroys the original offer.

oscoa_references: Hyde v Wrench (1840) 3 Beav 334

question: 'If an offer states that the offeree's silence will be treated as acceptance, the offeree's silence constitutes valid acceptance.' — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: English law does not permit the offeror to impose acceptance by silence. _Felthouse v Bindley_ (1862) 11 CBNS 869 — silence does not constitute acceptance.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. _Felthouse v Bindley_ (1862) established that an offeror cannot unilaterally impose acceptance terms. Silence is not acceptance, regardless of what the offer states.

oscoa_references: Felthouse v Bindley (1862) 11 CBNS 869

question: A coal merchant and a railway company have traded for years. The merchant sends a draft contract. The railway company never signs it but continues ordering and accepting coal deliveries under the same terms. Is there a contract?

options.text: No — the contract was never signed
options.feedback: Signature is not always required. Conduct can constitute acceptance.

options.text: Yes — the railway company's continued ordering and acceptance of deliveries constituted acceptance by conduct — _Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co_ (1877) 2 App Cas 666
options.feedback: Correct. _Brogden v Metropolitan Railway_ (1877) HL held that continuing to order and accept deliveries under the draft terms constituted acceptance by conduct.

options.text: No — the coal merchant must have specifically pointed to the draft contract on each delivery
options.feedback: A party may accept by acting in a way that objectively indicates assent, without formal acknowledgment.

options.text: Yes — but only from the date the draft contract was sent
options.feedback: The contract was formed when the railway company's conduct objectively demonstrated acceptance, not when the draft was sent.

oscoa_references: Brogden v Metropolitan Railway Co (1877) 2 App Cas 666

question: In _Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corporation_ [1955] 2 QB 327, the court held that for telex communications, acceptance is effective:

options.text: When the telex is sent from the offeree's office
options.feedback: The postal rule (acceptance on posting) applies to postal communications, not instantaneous ones.

options.text: When the telex is received by the offeror
options.feedback: Correct. For instantaneous communications, acceptance is governed by the receipt rule. The contract is formed where acceptance is received.

options.text: When the offeror reads the telex
options.feedback: The test is receipt into the offeror's system, not actual reading.

options.text: At the time the telex is dictated to the operator
options.feedback: Dictation is not sufficient; the communication must be transmitted and received.

oscoa_references: Entores Ltd v Miles Far East Corporation [1955] 2 QB 327

question: On 1 March, A posts an offer to B. B receives it on 3 March and posts acceptance on the same day. On 4 March, A calls B and says 'I revoke my offer.' On 5 March, A receives B's acceptance letter. Applying the postal rule, is there a contract?

options.text: Yes — acceptance was effective on posting (3 March), before revocation was communicated — _Adams v Lindsell_ (1818) 106 ER 250
options.feedback: Correct. Under the postal rule established in _Adams v Lindsell_, acceptance is effective when the letter is posted. The contract was formed on 3 March. A's purported revocation on 4 March came too late.

options.text: No — revocation was communicated on 4 March, before acceptance was received on 5 March
options.feedback: The postal rule makes acceptance effective on posting (3 March), not on receipt (5 March). The contract already existed when A tried to revoke.

options.text: Yes — revocation must be in writing to be effective
options.feedback: Oral revocation can be effective, but the postal rule means the contract was already formed before the revocation attempt.

options.text: No — the postal rule only applies if the offeree uses the same method of communication as the offeror
options.feedback: The postal rule does not impose a symmetry requirement — it applies whenever acceptance is posted.

oscoa_references: Adams v Lindsell (1818) 106 ER 250

question: An offer states: 'Acceptance must be by notice in writing to the offeror.' The offeree posts a letter of acceptance. The letter is never delivered. Under _Holwell Securities Ltd v Hughes_ [1974] 1 WLR 155, is there a contract?

options.text: Yes — the postal rule applies; acceptance was effective on posting
options.feedback: _Holwell Securities_ held that the postal rule was excluded by the phrase 'notice in writing to'.

options.text: No — the phrase 'notice in writing to' required actual communication of acceptance; the postal rule was excluded
options.feedback: Correct. _Holwell Securities Ltd v Hughes_ [1974] held that the offer's wording — requiring notice 'to' the offeror — excluded the postal rule. Actual communication was required.

options.text: Yes — 'notice in writing' includes postal communication
options.feedback: The Court focused on the word 'to' — requiring delivery, not just dispatch.

options.text: No — the postal rule never applies in property transactions
options.feedback: The postal rule can apply to property transactions in principle. The issue was the wording of the specific offer.

oscoa_references: Holwell Securities Ltd v Hughes [1974] 1 WLR 155
Adams v Lindsell (1818) 106 ER 250

question: In _Balfour v Balfour_ [1919] 2 KB 571, a husband working abroad promised to pay his wife £30 per month while she remained in England. The court held:

options.text: The husband was contractually bound — the promise was specific and certain
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal found the opposite. Domestic arrangements between spouses living together are presumptively not intended to create legal relations.

options.text: There was no enforceable contract — the agreement was a domestic arrangement with no intention to create legal relations
options.feedback: Correct. _Balfour v Balfour_ [1919] established the presumption that agreements between spouses in a domestic setting are not intended to be legally binding, absent evidence to the contrary.

options.text: The agreement was enforceable because consideration was provided by the wife in maintaining the household
options.feedback: The court did not find the necessary intention to create legal relations. Consideration alone is insufficient without intention.

options.text: The promise was void for uncertainty
options.feedback: The sum was certain. The issue was lack of contractual intention, not uncertainty.

oscoa_references: Balfour v Balfour [1919] 2 KB 571

question: A husband and wife separate. The husband signs a written agreement to transfer the house to the wife and pay her £40 per month, if she pays the mortgage. Each takes independent legal advice. The husband later refuses to transfer the house. Applying _Merritt v Merritt_ [1970] 1 WLR 1211, is the agreement enforceable?

options.text: No — spouses cannot contract with each other
options.feedback: Spouses can contract with each other. The question is whether there is intention to create legal relations.

options.text: Yes — the parties were separated, the agreement was in writing, and concerned property rights, rebutting the presumption against contractual intention
options.feedback: Correct. _Merritt v Merritt_ [1970] distinguished _Balfour v Balfour_: the parties were separated, the agreement was formalised in writing, and it concerned property rights rather than day-to-day domestic arrangements.

options.text: No — the agreement lacked consideration
options.feedback: The wife's undertaking to pay the mortgage provided sufficient consideration.

options.text: Yes — all agreements between spouses are legally binding
options.feedback: Not all — the starting point is the presumption in _Balfour v Balfour_, which can be rebutted on the facts as in _Merritt v Merritt_.

oscoa_references: Merritt v Merritt [1970] 1 WLR 1211
Balfour v Balfour [1919] 2 KB 571

question: A group of friends agree to share petrol costs on a road trip. One friend drives negligently and injures a passenger. The passenger can sue the driver for breach of the cost-sharing agreement. True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: Social arrangements between friends are presumed not to have contractual intention. _Coward v Motor Insurers' Bureau_ [1963] 1 QB 259.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. _Coward v MIB_ [1963] and _Albert v Motor Insurers' Bureau_ [1971] established that social and informal cost-sharing arrangements between friends or colleagues are presumed not to create legal relations. Any claim would lie in tort, not contract.

oscoa_references: Coward v Motor Insurers' Bureau [1963] 1 QB 259
Albert v Motor Insurers' Bureau [1971] 3 WLR 291

question: In _Esso Petroleum Co Ltd v Commissioners of Customs & Excise_ [1976] 1 WLR 1, Esso ran a promotion giving away World Cup coins with every purchase of four gallons of petrol. HMRC argued the coins were subject to purchase tax. The majority of the House of Lords held:

options.text: The coins were a gift — no contractual intention existed because the promotion was purely promotional
options.feedback: The majority held there was an intention to create legal relations in the commercial context.

options.text: There was an intention to create legal relations because the transaction occurred in a commercial context — a strong presumption in business settings
options.feedback: Correct. Lords Simon, Wilberforce and Fraser held that in a commercial setting there is a strong presumption of intention to create legal relations. The coins were supplied under a collateral contract.

options.text: The promotion created no legal obligations — it was a mere puff
options.feedback: This was the minority view. The majority found contractual intention in the commercial context.

options.text: The coins were a gift because no separate consideration was given for them
options.feedback: The majority found that the purchase of petrol supplied consideration for the coins under a collateral contract.

oscoa_references: Esso Petroleum Co Ltd v Commissioners of Customs & Excise [1976] 1 WLR 1

question: Two businesses sign a distribution agreement including the clause: This arrangement is not entered into as a formal or legal agreement and shall not be subject to legal jurisdiction in the law courts. A dispute arises and one party sues. Applying _Rose and Frank Co v JR Crompton & Bros Ltd_ [1925] AC 445, what is the effect of this clause?

options.text: The clause is void — all commercial agreements must be legally binding
options.feedback: Commercial parties can expressly negate contractual intention using appropriately drafted honour clauses.

options.text: The agreement is not legally binding — the honour clause successfully negated intention to create legal relations
options.feedback: Correct. _Rose and Frank Co v Crompton_ [1925] upheld the validity of an honour clause. This is a loophole — drafting express honour clauses to avoid binding obligations while maintaining commercial relationships.

options.text: The clause is effective only between sole traders, not companies
options.feedback: The _Rose and Frank_ case involved companies. The distinction between natural persons and corporate entities does not affect honour clauses.

options.text: The clause is void under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
options.feedback: UCTA 1977 controls exclusion and limitation clauses, not clauses that negate the existence of a contract altogether.

oscoa_references: Rose and Frank Co v JR Crompton & Bros Ltd [1925] AC 445

question: In _Kleinwort Benson Ltd v Malaysia Mining Corporation Bhd_ [1989] 1 WLR 379, a parent company issued a letter of comfort to a bank stating: It is our policy to ensure that our subsidiary is at all times in a position to meet its liabilities. The subsidiary later defaulted. The Court of Appeal held:

options.text: The letter was a binding guarantee — the parent company must pay
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal distinguished between a guarantee and a letter of comfort.

options.text: The letter was not legally binding — it was a statement of present policy, not a contractual promise
options.feedback: Correct. _Kleinwort Benson v MMC_ [1989] held that the words it is our policy indicated a present intention, not a contractual promise. The bank could have insisted on a formal guarantee but chose not to.

options.text: The letter created an estoppel preventing the parent company from denying liability
options.feedback: The court did not find the necessary elements for estoppel.

options.text: The letter was binding because it was given in a commercial context
options.feedback: While the commercial context created a presumption of intention, it was rebutted by the wording of the document itself.

oscoa_references: Kleinwort Benson Ltd v Malaysia Mining Corporation Bhd [1989] 1 WLR 379

question: In _Scammell and Nephew Ltd v Ouston_ [1941] AC 251, an agreement for the purchase of a van stated that payment would be on hire-purchase terms. The House of Lords held:

options.text: The contract was valid — hire-purchase terms had a customary meaning in the motor trade
options.feedback: The House of Lords found the term too vague to be enforced.

options.text: The term was void for vagueness — there were many different types of hire-purchase agreements, so the terms were insufficiently certain
options.feedback: Correct. _Scammell v Ouston_ [1941] established that where a term is so vague that the court cannot determine the parties' obligations with sufficient precision, the contract is void for uncertainty.

options.text: The contract was valid because a reasonable price could be implied by the court
options.feedback: The issue was not price but the nature of the transaction terms. Hire-purchase terms vary significantly.

options.text: The term was severable and the remainder of the contract was enforceable
options.feedback: The House of Lords found the term was so fundamental that it could not be severed.

oscoa_references: Scammell and Nephew Ltd v Ouston [1941] AC 251

question: A contract for the sale of machinery states: The price shall be agreed between the parties from time to time. No mechanism for determining the price if the parties disagree is specified. The parties later fail to agree on price. Under _May & Butcher Ltd v The King_ [1934] 2 KB 17, is the contract enforceable?

options.text: Yes — a reasonable price will be implied under the Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.8
options.feedback: Section 8 implies a reasonable price only where the contract is otherwise complete. An agreement to agree on price is not a complete contract.

options.text: No — an agreement to agree on an essential term such as price is not a binding contract; it is void for uncertainty
options.feedback: Correct. _May & Butcher v The King_ [1932/1934] (HL) held that an agreement to agree on a fundamental term such as price is not binding. This was reinforced in _Walford v Miles_ [1992] 2 AC 128.

options.text: Yes — the court can determine a fair price based on expert evidence
options.feedback: Courts will not rewrite contracts for the parties. Where no objective mechanism is stated, the court cannot supply one.

options.text: No — but only if one party acted in bad faith during negotiations
options.feedback: The contract is void for uncertainty regardless of the parties' good or bad faith.

oscoa_references: May & Butcher Ltd v The King [1934] 2 KB 17
Walford v Miles [1992] 2 AC 128

question: Where parties negotiate subject to contract, a binding contract may arise before formal documents are exchanged if they have agreed on all essential terms. True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: The label subject to contract creates a strong, albeit rebuttable, presumption that the parties do not intend to be bound until formal contracts are exchanged — _Tiverton Estates Ltd v Wearwell Ltd_ [1975] Ch 146.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. _Tiverton Estates v Wearwell_ [1975] established that subject to contract creates a strong presumption that no binding contract exists until formal exchange.

oscoa_references: Tiverton Estates Ltd v Wearwell Ltd [1975] Ch 146

question: A manufacturer and a supplier negotiate subject to contract. Before formal contracts are signed, the manufacturer asks the supplier to begin work. The supplier completes substantial work costing £1m before the deal collapses. Applying _RTS Flexible Systems Ltd v Molkerei Alois Muller GmbH & Co KG_ [2010] UKSC 14, what is the outcome?

options.text: No contract exists — all communications were subject to contract
options.feedback: The Supreme Court held that the subject to contract condition could be waived by conduct.

options.text: A contract was formed by the parties' conduct — they waived the subject to contract condition by proceeding with performance
options.feedback: Correct. _RTS Flexible Systems_ [2010] UKSC 14 established that where parties proceed with performance, they may waive a subject to contract condition. The Supreme Court looked at what a reasonable observer would conclude.

options.text: The supplier can claim only on a quantum meruit basis for the work done
options.feedback: While quantum meruit is a fallback, the Supreme Court found a contract existed based on the parties' conduct.

options.text: No contract — the supplier took the risk by beginning work without a signed contract
options.feedback: The Supreme Court rejected this argument. Where one party requests performance and the other performs, a contract may be inferred.

oscoa_references: RTS Flexible Systems Ltd v Molkerei Alois Muller GmbH & Co KG (UK Production) [2010] UKSC 14
Tiverton Estates Ltd v Wearwell Ltd [1975] Ch 146

question: A company drafts a lock-out agreement: The Seller agrees not to negotiate with any third party for the sale of the business for a reasonable period. Is this clause enforceable post-_Walford v Miles_ [1992] 2 AC 128?

options.text: Yes — reasonable period is an objective standard courts can determine
options.feedback: _Walford v Miles_ held that an agreement without a fixed duration is void for uncertainty.

options.text: No — the agreement must specify a fixed duration; a reasonable period is void for uncertainty following _Walford v Miles_
options.feedback: Correct. The loophole is to draft lock-out agreements with a fixed and express time limit (e.g., for a period of 6 months). _Walford v Miles_ [1992] requires a specified endpoint.

options.text: Yes — lock-out agreements are always enforceable in commercial contexts
options.feedback: _Walford v Miles_ specifically addressed the enforceability of lock-out agreements with a time limit requirement.

options.text: Yes — provided the reasonable period can be inferred from industry custom
options.feedback: The House of Lords rejected this argument. Without a fixed endpoint, neither party can know when their obligations end.

oscoa_references: Walford v Miles [1992] 2 AC 128

question: A customer signs a sales agreement without reading it. The agreement contains an exclusion clause in small print. The goods are defective. In _L'Estrange v F Graucob Ltd_ [1934] 2 KB 394, the Court of Appeal held:

options.text: The exclusion clause was not incorporated because the customer did not read it
options.feedback: _L'Estrange v Graucob_ held the opposite — a party is bound by their signature regardless of reading.

options.text: The customer was bound by the exclusion clause — a party who signs a contractual document is bound by its terms, whether or not they have read them
options.feedback: Correct. _L'Estrange v F Graucob Ltd_ [1934] 2 KB 394 established that signature signifies assent to all terms in the document.

options.text: The exclusion clause was void for unreasonableness under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
options.feedback: UCTA 1977 was enacted decades later and was not applicable to the _L'Estrange_ case. The issue was incorporation, not reasonableness.

options.text: The clause was not incorporated because it was not specifically drawn to the customer's attention
options.feedback: Where a document is signed, the general rule is that all terms are incorporated without specific notice — _L'Estrange v Graucob_.

oscoa_references: L'Estrange v F Graucob Ltd [1934] 2 KB 394

question: A driver enters a car park, takes a ticket from an automatic machine, and parks. The ticket refers to conditions displayed on the premises. A notice inside, not visible from the entrance, states the car park is not liable for injury to customers howsoever caused. The driver is injured due to the operator's negligence. Applying _Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking Ltd_ [1971] 2 QB 163, is the exclusion clause effective?

options.text: Yes — the ticket referred to conditions displayed on the premises
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal held that the notice was given after the contract was formed.

options.text: No — the contract was formed at the machine; the inside notice came too late and exclusion of personal injury is onerous, requiring the Red Hand rule
options.feedback: Correct. _Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking_ [1971]: the contract formed at the entrance. The internal notice was post-contractual. The exclusion of personal injury was onerous — _J Spurling Ltd v Bradshaw_ [1956] Red Hand rule.

options.text: Yes — the driver had an opportunity to read the notice after parking
options.feedback: Terms must be incorporated at or before the time of contracting. Opportunity after contracting is irrelevant.

options.text: No — UCTA 1977, s.2(1) prohibits all exclusion of personal injury liability
options.feedback: The primary issue was incorporation, not statutory control. Though UCTA 1977 s.2(1) would also defeat the clause.

oscoa_references: Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking Ltd [1971] 2 QB 163
J Spurling Ltd v Bradshaw [1956] 1 WLR 461

question: In _Hollier v Rambler Motors (AMC) Ltd_ [1972] 2 QB 71, the Court of Appeal held that ________ transactions over five years were insufficient to incorporate an exclusion clause by a course of dealing.
feedback: The correct answer is three or four. In _Hollier v Rambler Motors_ [1972], the court held that 3-4 transactions over five years did not establish a consistent course of dealing. Contrast _Kendall v Lillico_ [1969] 2 AC 31, where over 100 transactions were held sufficient.
oscoa_references: Hollier v Rambler Motors (AMC) Ltd [1972] 2 QB 71
Kendall v Lillico [1969] 2 AC 31

question: The plea of non est factum as established in _Saunders v Anglia Building Society_ [1971] AC 1004 requires:

options.text: That the signer did not receive independent legal advice before signing
options.feedback: The plea is not about legal advice. It concerns mistake as to the very nature of the document.

options.text: A radical difference between the document signed and what the signer believed it to be, and the signer must not have been careless
options.feedback: Correct. _Saunders v Anglia_ [1971] (HL) held the plea is available only where the document is radically different from what was believed and the signer exercised reasonable care.

options.text: That the signature was obtained by fraud or duress
options.feedback: Fraud and duress are separate doctrines. Non est factum concerns mistake.

options.text: That the contract involved the sale of land
options.feedback: Non est factum applies to all signed documents. In _Saunders v Anglia_, it concerned a mortgage.

oscoa_references: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: In _The Moorcock_ (1889) 14 PD 64, a shipowner contracted with a wharfinger to discharge cargo at a jetty. The vessel grounded and was damaged at low tide. The court implied a term that the berth was safe. What test did the court apply?

options.text: The term was implied because it was reasonable for the wharfinger to bear the risk
options.feedback: The test is necessity, not reasonableness — _Liverpool City Council v Irwin_ [1977] AC 239.

options.text: The officious bystander and business efficacy tests — the term was necessary to give the contract business efficacy and was so obvious it went without saying
options.feedback: Correct. _The Moorcock_ (1889) established both the business efficacy test and the officious bystander test: the term must be necessary to give the contract business efficacy and so obvious as to go without saying.

options.text: The term was implied by statute under the Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982
options.feedback: This was a common law implication in fact, not statutory. The SGSA 1982 post-dates _The Moorcock_ by nearly a century.

options.text: The term was implied based on custom and usage in the shipping industry
options.feedback: While custom can be a source of implied terms, _The Moorcock_ concerned implication in fact to give the specific contract business efficacy.

oscoa_references: The Moorcock (1889) 14 PD 64
Liverpool City Council v Irwin [1977] AC 239

question: A commercial lease is silent on whether the tenant is entitled to a rent refund if the lease is terminated early. The tenant argues a term should be implied. Applying _Marks and Spencer plc v BNP Paribas_ [2015] UKSC 72, how should the court decide?

options.text: The term will be implied — it is reasonable and fair to refund prepaid rent
options.feedback: The Supreme Court emphasised that reasonableness is not the test. The test is necessity.

options.text: A term will only be implied if it is necessary for business efficacy or so obvious as to go without saying — mere reasonableness is insufficient
options.feedback: Correct. _M&S v BNP Paribas_ [2015] restated the tests: the term must be necessary to give business efficacy or so obvious as to go without saying. On the facts, the Supreme Court refused to imply the term.

options.text: The term will be implied automatically in landlord-tenant relationships as a term implied in law
options.feedback: This was a question of implication in fact, not implication in law. The tenant sought a term specific to this contract.

options.text: The term will be implied if it reflects the parties' subjective intentions
options.feedback: The test is objective, not subjective. What matters is what reasonable parties would have agreed at the time.

oscoa_references: Marks and Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities Services Trust Co (Jersey) Ltd [2015] UKSC 72

question: In _Liverpool City Council v Irwin_ [1977] AC 239, the House of Lords implied a term into a lease that a landlord must take reasonable care to maintain common parts of a tower block. This is an example of:

options.text: A term implied in fact under the business efficacy test
options.feedback: The term was implied as a legal incident of the landlord-tenant relationship, not based on the specific facts of this contract.

options.text: A term implied in law — a standardised term applying to all contracts of a particular type (leases of multi-occupancy dwellings)
options.feedback: Correct. Terms implied in law are imposed on all contracts of a particular class (e.g., leases, employment), rather than being derived from specific facts. _Liverpool City Council v Irwin_ is the leading authority.

options.text: A term implied by statute under the Landlord and Tenant Act 1985
options.feedback: Though the LTA 1985 now imposes repairing obligations, the term was implied at common law in 1977.

options.text: A term implied by custom
options.feedback: The term was implied as a matter of law, not custom. It derived from the nature of the relationship itself.

oscoa_references: Liverpool City Council v Irwin [1977] AC 239

question: A commercial contract states: This agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and excludes all implied terms whether by statute or common law to the fullest extent permitted by law. How does this affect the implication of terms, applying _Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corp v Texaco Ltd_ [2003] EWHC 1964?

options.text: The entire agreement clause is ineffective — terms can always be implied by the court
options.feedback: Entire agreement clauses can effectively exclude implied terms, subject to statutory controls.

options.text: It makes it harder to imply a term but clear words are needed to exclude all implied terms — and statutory implied terms that are non-excludable (e.g., CRA 2015) remain
options.feedback: Correct. _Exxonmobil v Texaco_ [2003] held that an entire agreement clause makes it harder to imply terms but does not automatically exclude them. Certain statutory implied terms cannot be excluded at all (e.g., CRA 2015 ss.9-11 in consumer contracts).

options.text: An entire agreement clause automatically excludes all implied terms without exception
options.feedback: Statutory terms that are mandatory cannot be excluded even by an entire agreement clause.

options.text: An entire agreement clause only relates to express terms and has no effect on implied terms
options.feedback: Entire agreement clauses typically cover both express and implied terms and affect the court's willingness to imply terms.

oscoa_references: Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm)

question: [Gate C2] Which factor was decisive in _Merritt v Merritt_ [1970] for finding contractual intention where _Balfour v Balfour_ [1919] found none?

options.text: The presence of valuable consideration
options.feedback: Consideration existed but was not the decisive factor distinguishing the cases.

options.text: The parties were separated and the agreement was formalised in writing, rebutting the domestic presumption
options.feedback: Correct. _Merritt v Merritt_ [1970] held that separation and a formal written agreement about property rights rebutted the presumption against contractual intention.

options.text: The agreement involved land, which always requires contractual intention
options.feedback: While land transactions carry a stronger inference, the nature of the subject matter was not decisive.

options.text: Both parties had independent legal advice
options.feedback: Legal advice is relevant but not decisive. The key distinction was separation and formality.

oscoa_references: Merritt v Merritt [1970] 1 WLR 1211
Balfour v Balfour [1919] 2 KB 571

question: [Gate C2] A commercial agreement states: This is a gentlemen's agreement, binding in honour only, and shall not give rise to any legal obligations. The parties perform for two years before a dispute. Is there an enforceable contract?

options.text: Yes — two years of performance creates a contract by conduct
options.feedback: An express honour clause negates contractual intention from the outset. Performance does not override it.

options.text: No — the honour clause successfully negates intention following _Rose and Frank Co v JR Crompton & Bros Ltd_ [1925] AC 445
options.feedback: Correct. Parties may expressly exclude legal relations using an honour clause, even in commercial contexts.

options.text: Yes — the court will strike down the honour clause as contrary to public policy
options.feedback: English law respects party autonomy. Honour clauses are not per se contrary to public policy.

options.text: No — because the agreement lacked consideration
options.feedback: The issue is intention to create legal relations, not consideration.

oscoa_references: Rose and Frank Co v JR Crompton & Bros Ltd [1925] AC 445

question: [Gate C2] A contract states: The price for the remaining stock shall be agreed between the parties later. No mechanism resolves a failure to agree. The parties disagree on price. Is the term enforceable?

options.text: Yes — the court will determine a reasonable price under SGA 1979, s.8
options.feedback: Section 8 applies only where the contract is otherwise complete, not where the parties have expressly left price for later agreement.

options.text: No — an agreement to agree on an essential term is void for uncertainty: _May & Butcher Ltd v The King_ [1934]
options.feedback: Correct. _May & Butcher_ held that an agreement to agree on price is not a binding contract.

options.text: Yes — the parties are bound to negotiate in good faith
options.feedback: English law does not recognise a general duty to negotiate in good faith — _Walford v Miles_ [1992].

options.text: Yes — the remainder of the contract is valid and the price term can be severed
options.feedback: Where the uncertain term goes to the heart of the contract (price), the entire contract may fail.

oscoa_references: May & Butcher Ltd v The King [1934] 2 KB 17

question: [Gate C2] A party signs a contract containing an onerous exclusion clause without reading it. The other party did not draw the clause to their attention. Under _L'Estrange v F Graucob Ltd_ [1934] 2 KB 394, is the clause binding?

options.text: No — the clause was not specifically brought to the signing party's attention
options.feedback: The Red Hand rule applies to unsigned documents. A signature generally binds regardless.

options.text: Yes — a party is bound by their signature to a contractual document, whether or not they read it
options.feedback: Correct. _L'Estrange v Graucob_ [1934] established that signature binds the signatory to all terms regardless of reading.

options.text: No — UCTA 1977 requires all exclusion clauses to be individually negotiated
options.feedback: UCTA 1977 imposes a reasonableness test, not an individual negotiation requirement.

options.text: No — signature only binds if the signatory had a reasonable opportunity to read
options.feedback: The opportunity to read is always present when a document is presented for signature. Choosing not to read is not a defence.

oscoa_references: L'Estrange v F Graucob Ltd [1934] 2 KB 394

question: [Gate C2] In _Interfoto Picture Library Ltd v Stiletto Visual Programmes Ltd_ [1989] QB 433, a delivery note contained a £5 per transparency per day holding charge. The client did not read it. The Court of Appeal held:

options.text: The clause was incorporated — the delivery note was a contractual document
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal found the clause was not incorporated because it was unusually onerous.

options.text: The clause was not incorporated — it was unusually onerous and required greater notice under the extended Red Hand rule
options.feedback: Correct. _Interfoto v Stiletto_ [1989] extended the Red Hand rule. Onerous terms in unsigned documents require specific notice beyond what is reasonable for standard terms.

options.text: The clause was incorporated but void as a penalty
options.feedback: The penalty doctrine was not the basis of the decision. The clause failed at the incorporation stage.

options.text: The clause was incorporated but unreasonable under UCTA 1977
options.feedback: The court did not reach UCTA 1977. The clause failed at incorporation.

oscoa_references: Interfoto Picture Library Ltd v Stiletto Visual Programmes Ltd [1989] QB 433
J Spurling Ltd v Bradshaw [1956] 1 WLR 461

question: [Gate C2] The officious bystander test for implied terms asks whether the term is:

options.text: Reasonable and fair in all the circumstances
options.feedback: Reasonableness and fairness are not the test. Necessity and obviousness are.

options.text: So obvious that if an officious bystander suggested it, both parties would reply 'Oh, of course!'
options.feedback: Correct. The officious bystander test from _The Moorcock_ (1889) and restated in _M&S v BNP Paribas_ [2015] asks whether the term is so obvious it goes without saying.

options.text: Expressly discussed during contract negotiations
options.feedback: If the term were expressly discussed, it would be an express term. The implication of terms deals with what was not discussed but must have been intended.

options.text: Customary in the relevant trade or industry
options.feedback: Custom is a separate ground for implying terms. The officious bystander test is about what these specific parties would have agreed.

oscoa_references: The Moorcock (1889) 14 PD 64
Marks and Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities Services Trust Co (Jersey) Ltd [2015] UKSC 72

question: [Gate C2] A mother promises her daughter: If you give up your job and come to London to study for the Bar, I will pay you a monthly allowance. The daughter does so but they later fall out. Is the promise binding?

options.text: Yes — the daughter provided consideration by giving up her job
options.feedback: Even with consideration, intention to create legal relations is a separate requirement.

options.text: No — applying _Jones v Padavatton_ [1969] 1 WLR 328, family arrangements are presumed not legally binding, especially between parent and child
options.feedback: Correct. _Jones v Padavatton_ [1969] applied the _Balfour v Balfour_ principle to parent-child arrangements. Domestic arrangements based on love and affection are not contracts.

options.text: Yes — the agreement was specific enough to be enforced
options.feedback: Specificity goes to certainty, not intention. Even a certain promise may lack contractual intention in a family setting.

options.text: Yes — the daughter suffered a detriment by giving up her job
options.feedback: Detriment and reliance are relevant to consideration and estoppel but do not by themselves establish intention to contract.

oscoa_references: Jones v Padavatton [1969] 1 WLR 328
Balfour v Balfour [1919] 2 KB 571

question: [Gate C2] In _RTS Flexible Systems Ltd v Molkerei Alois Muller GmbH_ [2010] UKSC 14, the Supreme Court held that a subject to contract stipulation:

options.text: Is always effective to prevent formation until formal documents are exchanged
options.feedback: The Supreme Court held the condition can be waived by the parties' conduct.

options.text: Can be waived by the parties' conduct if they proceed to perform as though it were binding
options.feedback: Correct. _RTS Flexible Systems_ [2010] held that the condition is not immutable; where the parties proceed with performance, a reasonable observer would conclude they waived it.

options.text: Is overridden by any subsequent oral agreement
options.feedback: Waiver requires conduct or words objectively demonstrating an intention to be bound, not merely a subsequent oral agreement.

options.text: Is ineffective in commercial contracts — it only applies to consumer contracts
options.feedback: Subject to contract is effective in all contracts. The case involved two commercial entities.

oscoa_references: RTS Flexible Systems Ltd v Molkerei Alois Muller GmbH & Co KG (UK Production) [2010] UKSC 14

question: [Gate C2] In _Malik v BCCI_ [1998] AC 20, the House of Lords implied a duty of mutual trust and confidence. What is its legal basis?

options.text: A term implied in fact from the officious bystander test
options.feedback: The term was not derived from the specific facts of the Malik contract but applied to all employment contracts.

options.text: A term implied in law — a standardised incident of all employment contracts
options.feedback: Correct. _Malik v BCCI_ [1998] held the duty of mutual trust and confidence is a term implied in law in every employment contract.

options.text: An express term of the employment contract
options.feedback: The term was not expressed in the contract. It was implied as a matter of law.

options.text: A statutory implied term under the Employment Rights Act 1996
options.feedback: The term was implied at common law, not by statute.

oscoa_references: Malik v Bank of Credit and Commerce International SA [1998] AC 20

question: [Gate C2] What distinguishes a binding guarantee from a non-binding letter of comfort following _Kleinwort Benson v MMC_ [1989]?

options.text: The document must be headed Guarantee
options.feedback: The label is not decisive. The substance of the promise is what matters.

options.text: The document must contain promissory language of contractual obligation, not merely statements of policy or present intention
options.feedback: Correct. _Kleinwort Benson_ [1989]: it is our policy indicated present intention, not contractual promise. A guarantee requires promissory language.

options.text: A letter of comfort is binding if given in a commercial context
options.feedback: Even in commercial contexts, the presumption of intention may be rebutted by non-promissory wording.

options.text: A letter of comfort is binding if the creditor relied on it
options.feedback: Reliance is relevant to estoppel but does not convert a non-promissory statement into a contractual promise.

oscoa_references: Kleinwort Benson Ltd v Malaysia Mining Corporation Bhd [1989] 1 WLR 379

question: [Gate C2] A hotel guest books at reception. In the bedroom, a notice states the hotel is not liable for loss of guests' property. Jewellery is stolen. Applying _Olley v Marlborough Court Ltd_ [1949] 1 KB 532, is the clause effective?

options.text: Yes — the guest had notice of the clause before the theft
options.feedback: Notice after the contract is formed is irrelevant. The contract formed at reception.

options.text: No — the contract was formed at reception; the bedroom notice came after the contract was made and was not incorporated
options.feedback: Correct. _Olley v Marlborough Court_ [1949]: terms must be incorporated at the time of contracting. The bedroom notice was post-contractual.

options.text: Yes — the notice was displayed in a prominent position
options.feedback: Prominence is relevant but timing is equally critical. The notice was not present at the point of contracting.

options.text: No — UCTA 1977 prohibits exclusion of liability for theft
options.feedback: UCTA 1977 does not prohibit exclusion of theft liability. The clause failed at incorporation, not statutory control.

oscoa_references: Olley v Marlborough Court Ltd [1949] 1 KB 532

question: [Gate C2] When can the court sever an uncertain clause and enforce the remainder of the contract?

options.text: Whenever the uncertain clause is not the main purpose
options.feedback: The test is more nuanced. Severance is not available as a matter of course.

options.text: If the clause can be deleted without altering the remaining text and the contract still makes sense without it — but not if the clause is fundamental
options.feedback: Correct. Severance (the blue pencil test: _Tillman v Egon Zehnder_ [2019]) allows removal of words if the remainder makes grammatical sense without rearrangement.

options.text: Severance is never available — the entire contract is void
options.feedback: Severance is available where the uncertain part is not fundamental and can be cleanly removed.

options.text: The court can rewrite the clause to make it workable
options.feedback: The court cannot rewrite contracts. Severance removes, not rewrites.

oscoa_references: Tillman v Egon Zehnder Ltd [2019] UKSC 32

question: [Gate C2] The parol evidence rule, as stated in _Jacobs v Batavia and General Plantations Trust Ltd_ [1924] 1 Ch 287, provides that:

options.text: Oral evidence may always be given to vary a written contract
options.feedback: That is the opposite of the parol evidence rule.

options.text: Extrinsic evidence is not admissible to add to, vary, or contradict the terms of a written contract
options.feedback: Correct. The parol evidence rule limits the use of extrinsic evidence (oral statements, prior drafts, negotiations) to vary a written contract.

options.text: All prior oral negotiations are admissible if both parties agree
options.feedback: The parol evidence rule specifically excludes such evidence, regardless of party agreement.

options.text: The rule applies only to contracts for the sale of land
options.feedback: The parol evidence rule applies to all written contracts, not just land contracts.

oscoa_references: Jacobs v Batavia and General Plantations Trust Ltd [1924] 1 Ch 287

question: [Gate C2] A seller agrees with a buyer that for a reasonable time it will not negotiate with third parties for the sale of its business. The buyer incurs costs preparing a bid. The seller sells to a third party after 3 months. Applying _Walford v Miles_ [1992], what is the buyer's position?

options.text: The buyer can claim damages — 3 months was clearly insufficient
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that an agreement without a fixed time limit is void for uncertainty.

options.text: The lock-out agreement is unenforceable because it lacked a fixed duration — the buyer has no contractual claim
options.feedback: Correct. _Walford v Miles_ [1992]: a lock-out must specify a fixed duration. The loophole is to draft with an express term (e.g., 6 months). Without it, no remedy.

options.text: The buyer can recover wasted costs in restitution
options.feedback: Restitution for pre-contractual expenditure is not generally available. No unjust enrichment occurred.

options.text: The buyer can seek specific performance of the lock-out
options.feedback: If void for uncertainty, no equitable remedy. Specific performance would require enforcing a duty to negotiate — which English law will not do.

oscoa_references: Walford v Miles [1992] 2 AC 128

question: [Gate C2] A customer deposits a bag at a cloakroom, pays 2d and receives a ticket stating See back. On the back are conditions limiting liability to £10. The bag worth £100 is lost. The customer did not read the back. Applying _Parker v South Eastern Railway_ (1877), what is the position?

options.text: The conditions are not binding — the customer did not read them
options.feedback: The test is whether reasonable steps were taken to bring terms to the customer's attention, not whether the customer actually read them.

options.text: The conditions are incorporated — See back constitutes reasonable notice of the terms
options.feedback: Correct. _Parker v South Eastern Railway_ (1877): where a ticket contains a clear reference to conditions, reasonable notice has been given. The customer is bound even if they chose not to read.

options.text: The conditions are not incorporated — customers cannot be expected to read tickets
options.feedback: The Court held the opposite. A reasonable person receiving a ticket with See back would understand it refers to conditions.

options.text: The limitation to £10 was unreasonable and therefore not incorporated
options.feedback: At the time of _Parker_, reasonableness was not a requirement for incorporation. The sole issue was reasonable notice.

oscoa_references: Parker v South Eastern Railway Co (1877) 2 CPD 416

question: [Gate C2] Drafting an entire agreement clause stating: All implied terms are excluded to the fullest extent permitted by law. What is the key limitation?

options.text: It cannot exclude mandatory statutory implied terms, such as CRA 2015 ss.9-11 in consumer contracts or UCTA 1977 s.2(1)
options.feedback: Correct. While an entire agreement clause can restrict implied terms (_Exxonmobil v Texaco_ [2003]), it cannot override mandatory statutory protections.

options.text: It is completely ineffective — all implied terms survive
options.feedback: This overstates the limitation. Entire agreement clauses are generally effective against implied terms in fact, subject to statutory limits.

options.text: It can exclude all implied terms without exception
options.feedback: Certain statutory terms are non-excludable by law, and fraud always vitiates such clauses.

options.text: It only excludes terms implied by custom and usage
options.feedback: A well-drafted entire agreement clause can cover common law implied terms as well, subject to statutory limits.

oscoa_references: Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm)
Consumer Rights Act 2015, s.31
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.2(1)

question: [Gate C2] A sale of goods contract does not specify the price. Under SGA 1979, s.8(2):

options.text: The contract is void for uncertainty
options.feedback: Section 8 provides a statutory safety net for price uncertainty.

options.text: The buyer must pay a reasonable price — what is reasonable is a question of fact
options.feedback: Correct. SGA 1979, s.8(2): where the price is not determined, a reasonable price is implied. This is a statutory mechanism for resolving uncertainty.

options.text: The contract is voidable at either party's option
options.feedback: Section 8 does not make the contract voidable. It fills the gap with an objective standard.

options.text: The seller may fix any price it chooses
options.feedback: The price must be reasonable, not arbitrary. The court retains control over reasonableness.

oscoa_references: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.8(2)

question: [Gate C2] Under TULR(C)A 1992, s.179, collective agreements between employers and trade unions are:

options.text: Always legally binding contracts
options.feedback: The statute creates the opposite presumption.

options.text: Presumed not to be legally binding unless they contain a provision stating the parties intend them to be enforceable
options.feedback: Correct. TULR(C)A 1992, s.179 reverses the normal commercial presumption. Collective agreements are presumed not to be legally enforceable.

options.text: Only binding if registered with the Central Arbitration Committee
options.feedback: There is no registration requirement under TULR(C)A 1992.

options.text: Binding on individual employees but not on the trade union
options.feedback: The presumption applies to the collective agreement as a whole, not selectively.

oscoa_references: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992, s.179

question: [Gate C2] In _British Crane Hire v Ipswich Plant Hire_ [1975] QB 303, an exclusion clause was incorporated by course of dealing. Which factor was decisive?

options.text: The parties had contracted twice before — two transactions were sufficient
options.feedback: Number alone is not decisive — two prior transactions may not be enough (_Hollier v Rambler Motors_).

options.text: Both parties were in the same trade, knew the standard conditions, and had used them consistently on prior occasions
options.feedback: Correct. _British Crane Hire_ [1975]: trade practice combined with consistent prior use was sufficient to incorporate terms.

options.text: The defendant signed a document incorporating the clause on a previous occasion
options.feedback: There was no signed document. Incorporation was by conduct and trade practice.

options.text: The clause was specifically discussed during oral contract negotiations
options.feedback: The clause was not discussed. Incorporation was by the course of prior dealings and trade knowledge.

oscoa_references: British Crane Hire Corporation Ltd v Ipswich Plant Hire Ltd [1975] QB 303
Hollier v Rambler Motors (AMC) Ltd [1972] 2 QB 71

question: Under SGA 1979, s.14(2), goods must be of satisfactory quality. Which of the following is NOT a factor listed in s.14(2B) for assessing this?

options.text: Fitness for all purposes for which goods of that kind are commonly supplied
options.feedback: This is listed in s.14(2B)(a).

options.text: Appearance, finish, freedom from minor defects, safety, and durability
options.feedback: These are all listed in s.14(2B)(b)-(e).

options.text: The price paid for the goods
options.feedback: Correct. Price is referenced in s.14(2A) as part of the overall assessment but is not a listed factor in s.14(2B).

options.text: Freedom from minor defects
options.feedback: This is listed in s.14(2B)(c).

oscoa_references: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.14(2A)-(2B)

question: A buyer tells a car dealer: I need a vehicle that can tow a 2-tonne trailer. The dealer recommends a model. The buyer purchases it but discovers it cannot safely tow 2 tonnes. Under SGA 1979, s.14(3), what is the position?

options.text: The buyer has no claim unless the dealer gave an express guarantee
options.feedback: Section 14(3) is an implied term. No express guarantee is needed.

options.text: The buyer has a claim — the particular purpose was made known and the buyer relied on the dealer's skill and judgment
options.feedback: Correct. SGA 1979, s.14(3): where the buyer makes known the particular purpose and relies on the seller's skill, there is an implied condition of fitness — even if the goods are satisfactory for general use.

options.text: The buyer has no claim — the car was satisfactory for normal driving
options.feedback: Fitness for a particular purpose under s.14(3) is separate from satisfactory quality under s.14(2). The car may fail one test but pass the other.

options.text: The buyer has no claim — the purpose was not stated in writing
options.feedback: Section 14(3) does not require writing. Purpose may be made known orally or by implication.

oscoa_references: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.14(3)

question: Under CRA 2015, s.22, a consumer who buys goods that are not of satisfactory quality has a short-term right to reject within how many days?

options.text: 7 days
options.feedback: The CRA 2015 provides a longer period than 7 days.

options.text: 14 days
options.feedback: The 14-day period under CCR 2013 relates to distance selling cancellation, not rejection for non-conformity.

options.text: 30 days
options.feedback: Correct. CRA 2015, s.22: 30 days from the day after delivery or transfer of ownership. After 30 days, the consumer moves to repair/replacement (s.23) then price reduction/final rejection (s.24).

options.text: 6 months
options.feedback: 6 months is the period during which non-conformity is presumed to have existed at delivery (s.19(14)), not the rejection window.

oscoa_references: Consumer Rights Act 2015, ss.22-24

question: Under CRA 2015, s.49, a trader supplying a service to a consumer must perform the service with reasonable care and skill. True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: Correct. CRA 2015, s.49 implies a term that services will be performed with reasonable care and skill. This replicates SGA 1982, s.13 for consumer contracts.

options.text: False
options.feedback: The requirement is statutory. CRA 2015, s.49 expressly implies this term into all consumer service contracts.

oscoa_references: Consumer Rights Act 2015, s.49
Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982, s.13

question: A business customer signs a contract containing: The supplier shall not be liable for any loss or damage howsoever caused. The customer argues the clause should not be binding because it was in small print. Applying _L'Estrange v Graucob_ [1934], what is the position?

options.text: The clause is not binding — small print requires specific notice under the Red Hand rule
options.feedback: The Red Hand rule applies to unsigned documents. A signature incorporates all terms.

options.text: The clause is incorporated by the signature — but its effectiveness then depends on statutory controls under UCTA 1977
options.feedback: Correct. Signature incorporates the clause (_L'Estrange_), but it must then survive UCTA 1977 reasonableness (ss.3, 6, 11).

options.text: The clause is void as a matter of law
options.feedback: Exclusion of all liability is not per se void. It may be unreasonable under UCTA 1977.

options.text: The clause is binding and effective without further scrutiny
options.feedback: Signature defeats incorporation arguments but not statutory controls. UCTA 1977 may still apply.

oscoa_references: L'Estrange v F Graucob Ltd [1934] 2 KB 394
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, ss.3, 6, 11

question: In _J Spurling Ltd v Bradshaw_ [1956] 1 WLR 461, Denning LJ stated that an onerous or unusual exclusion clause must be brought to the other party's attention with a '________ hand' pointing to it.
feedback: The correct answer is 'red'. The Red Hand rule: the more onerous or unusual a term, the greater the notice required. It must be drawn to the other party's attention in a way that makes it stand out. Applied in _Interfoto v Stiletto_ [1989] and _Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking_ [1971].
oscoa_references: J Spurling Ltd v Bradshaw [1956] 1 WLR 461
Interfoto Picture Library Ltd v Stiletto Visual Programmes Ltd [1989] QB 433

question: The contra proferentem rule, applied in _Canada Steamship Lines Ltd v The King_ [1952] AC 192, means:

options.text: Exclusion clauses are void as a matter of law
options.feedback: The rule is one of construction, not invalidity.

options.text: Ambiguous exclusion clauses are construed against the party seeking to rely on them
options.feedback: Correct. Contra proferentem: if the clause is ambiguous, the interpretation less favourable to the proferens prevails. _Canada Steamship_ set out a three-stage test for excluding negligence.

options.text: Exclusion clauses must be written in plain English
options.feedback: Plain English is a separate transparency requirement under CRA 2015, s.68, not the contra proferentem rule.

options.text: Exclusion clauses are only enforceable in B2B contracts
options.feedback: Exclusion clauses can be enforceable in consumer contracts too, subject to statutory controls. Contra proferentem applies regardless.

oscoa_references: Canada Steamship Lines Ltd v The King [1952] AC 192

question: A contract clause states: The Supplier shall not be liable for any loss or damage to the Customer's property howsoever arising. The Supplier's employee negligently causes a fire destroying the Customer's property. Applying _Canada Steamship_ guidelines, is negligence excluded?

options.text: Yes — howsoever arising is wide enough to cover negligence under limb 2 of the _Canada Steamship_ test
options.feedback: Correct. Under _Canada Steamship_ limb 2, words wide enough to cover negligence can do so even without express reference. _Monarch Airlines v London Luton_ [1997] confirmed howsoever arising is sufficient.

options.text: No — the clause does not expressly mention negligence
options.feedback: Express reference is limb 1. Limb 2 allows sufficiently wide words to cover negligence without express mention.

options.text: No — exclusion of negligence is prohibited by UCTA 1977, s.2(2)
options.feedback: UCTA 1977 s.2(2) subjects such exclusion to the reasonableness test but does not prohibit it absolutely.

options.text: No — contra proferentem requires construction against the supplier
options.feedback: Where the words are unambiguous and broad enough, contra proferentem does not override the natural meaning.

oscoa_references: Canada Steamship Lines Ltd v The King [1952] AC 192
Monarch Airlines Ltd v London Luton Airport Ltd [1997] CLC 698

question: UCTA 1977, s.2(1) provides that a person cannot exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. This prohibition is:

options.text: Subject to the reasonableness test under s.11
options.feedback: The reasonableness test does not apply to s.2(1). The prohibition is absolute.

options.text: Absolute — the exclusion is void regardless of reasonableness or consent
options.feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977, s.2(1) is an absolute prohibition. No exclusion or restriction of liability for death or personal injury from negligence is valid, regardless of consent.

options.text: Only applicable to consumer contracts
options.feedback: Section 2(1) applies to all contracts, business and consumer alike.

options.text: Waivable by express agreement in writing
options.feedback: The prohibition cannot be waived. Section 2(1) overrides any contractual agreement to the contrary.

oscoa_references: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.2(1)

question: In _Photo Production Ltd v Securicor Transport Ltd_ [1980] AC 827, a security guard deliberately started a fire destroying the factory. The contract excluded liability for any injurious act by Securicor's employees. The House of Lords held:

options.text: Fundamental breach automatically prevents reliance on the exclusion clause — Securicor was liable
options.feedback: This was the rule of law approach the House of Lords explicitly overruled.

options.text: Whether the exclusion clause covers fundamental breach is a matter of construction — the court must determine if the clause, on its true meaning, extends to the breach
options.feedback: Correct. _Photo Production v Securicor_ [1980]: no rule of law prevents reliance on exclusion clauses for fundamental breach. It is a matter of construction. The clause must then survive UCTA 1977.

options.text: Deliberate acts cannot be excluded — Securicor was liable as a matter of public policy
options.feedback: The House of Lords rejected a public policy rule. The question was whether the clause covered the employee's deliberate act.

options.text: The exclusion clause was void under UCTA 1977
options.feedback: UCTA 1977 was not argued. The case concerned the common law doctrine of fundamental breach.

oscoa_references: Photo Production Ltd v Securicor Transport Ltd [1980] AC 827

question: Under CRA 2015, s.62, a term in a consumer contract is unfair if:

options.text: The trader has a dominant market position
options.feedback: Market dominance is not the test. Unfairness is assessed by reference to good faith and significant imbalance.

options.text: Contrary to the requirement of good faith, it causes a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the consumer's detriment
options.feedback: Correct. CRA 2015, s.62(4): unfairness requires both (a) contrary to good faith and (b) significant imbalance to the consumer's detriment.

options.text: The consumer did not read the term before signing
options.feedback: Failure to read is relevant to incorporation, not unfairness. The test in s.62 is objective.

options.text: The term was not individually negotiated
options.feedback: Lack of individual negotiation is an indicator but not determinative. The test is the significant imbalance test.

oscoa_references: Consumer Rights Act 2015, s.62

question: A consumer phone contract states: £25/month. In hidden small print, the price rises annually by RPI + 3.9%. The consumer challenges as unfair. The trader argues the price is a core term exempt under CRA 2015, s.64. What is the correct position?

options.text: The price variation is a core term and cannot be assessed for fairness
options.feedback: Core terms are only exempt if transparent and prominent.

options.text: The term can be assessed for fairness — the core term exemption only applies if the term is transparent and prominent; buried price increases fail the transparency test
options.feedback: Correct. CRA 2015, s.64 excludes core terms only if transparent and prominent. Hidden price mechanisms fail the transparency requirement (s.68) and can be assessed. Schedule 2 lists price variation as potentially unfair.

options.text: The entire contract is void
options.feedback: The remainder continues if capable of operating without the unfair term — s.67.

options.text: The clause is automatically unfair under Schedule 2
options.feedback: Schedule 2 is an indicative grey list, not automatic prohibition. Terms must be assessed individually.

oscoa_references: Consumer Rights Act 2015, ss.62, 64, 68
Consumer Rights Act 2015, Schedule 2

question: In _ICS v West Bromwich BS_ [1998] 1 WLR 896, Lord Hoffmann's first principle of contractual interpretation states that interpretation is:

options.text: The ascertainment of the parties' subjective intentions
options.feedback: English law takes an objective approach, not a subjective one.

options.text: The meaning the document would convey to a reasonable person with all background knowledge available to the parties at the time of the contract
options.feedback: Correct. Lord Hoffmann: interpretation is objective, incorporating the factual matrix — all background knowledge reasonably available to the parties.

options.text: The literal dictionary meaning of each word
options.feedback: Lord Hoffmann's approach incorporates context. The literal meaning is one consideration but not the sole determinant.

options.text: Always the interpretation most favourable to the party who did not draft the contract
options.feedback: Contra proferentem is a secondary rule of last resort, not the primary principle.

oscoa_references: Investors Compensation Scheme Ltd v West Bromwich Building Society [1998] 1 WLR 896

question: A building contract's guarantee clause is ambiguous about whether all or only some advance payments are refundable on termination. Applying _Rainy Sky v Kookmin Bank_ [2011] and _Arnold v Britton_ [2015], which approach should the court take?

options.text: Always prefer the interpretation that achieves business common sense, even if it contradicts the words
options.feedback: _Arnold v Britton_ [2015] cautioned that commercial common sense must not override the language the parties used.

options.text: Apply the natural meaning in context; where two interpretations are possible, prefer one consistent with business common sense, while respecting the primacy of the language
options.feedback: Correct. _Rainy Sky_ [2011]: prefer business common sense where ambiguity exists. _Arnold v Britton_ [2015]: do not undervalue the language chosen. The court balances both.

options.text: Always prefer the interpretation most favourable to the guarantor
options.feedback: There is no presumptive rule favouring guarantors. Ordinary principles of interpretation apply.

options.text: Apply the literal meaning of each word in isolation
options.feedback: Words are interpreted in their contractual and factual context, not in isolation.

oscoa_references: Rainy Sky SA v Kookmin Bank [2011] UKSC 50
Arnold v Britton [2015] UKSC 36

question: In _MWB v Rock Advertising_ [2018] UKSC 24, the Supreme Court held that a No Oral Modification (NOM) clause:

options.text: Is void — parties are always free to vary their contract orally
options.feedback: The Supreme Court held the opposite — NOM clauses are legally effective.

options.text: Is legally effective; an oral variation in breach of a NOM clause is invalid unless both parties intended to and did waive the NOM requirement
options.feedback: Correct. _MWB v Rock Advertising_ [2018]: NOM clauses are effective. Oral modifications are invalid without compliance, unless the parties mutually waive the clause.

options.text: Is only effective if individually negotiated
options.feedback: The Supreme Court did not impose an individual negotiation requirement. The clause is effective in standard form contracts.

options.text: Only applies to consumer contracts
options.feedback: _MWB v Rock Advertising_ was a commercial contract, but the principle applies to all contracts.

oscoa_references: MWB Business Exchange Centres Ltd v Rock Advertising Ltd [2018] UKSC 24

question: An investment contract states: The Investor acknowledges it has not relied on any representation not in this agreement. The investor now claims negligent misrepresentation. Applying _Peekay Intermark v ANZ_ [2006] EWCA Civ 386, what is the effect?

options.text: The clause is void under MA 1967, s.3
options.feedback: Section 3 subjects such clauses to the reasonableness test, not automatic voidness.

options.text: The clause creates contractual estoppel — the investor is prevented from asserting reliance, provided the clause satisfies the UCTA reasonableness test and there is no fraud
options.feedback: Correct. _Peekay v ANZ_ [2006]: a non-reliance clause creates contractual estoppel, preventing the party from claiming reliance on pre-contractual representations. MA 1967, s.3 requires reasonableness. Fraud is an exception.

options.text: The clause is ineffective — oral representations always override written terms
