options.feedback: This contradicts _Peekay Intermark_. Non-reliance clauses can be effective as contractual estoppels.

options.text: The investor can always rescind regardless of the clause
options.feedback: If the non-reliance clause is reasonable, it may prevent a misrepresentation claim because reliance cannot be established.

oscoa_references: Peekay Intermark Ltd v Australia and New Zealand Banking Group Ltd [2006] EWCA Civ 386
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3

question: In _Edgington v Fitzmaurice_ (1885) 29 Ch D 459, directors stated debentures would fund expansion, but intended to pay debts. The court held a statement of future intention:

options.text: Cannot constitute a misrepresentation
options.feedback: A statement of future intention can be a misrepresentation if the stated intention was not genuinely held.

options.text: Is a misrepresentation if the represented state of mind (the intention) was not genuinely held — a statement of intention involves a representation of existing fact (state of mind)
options.feedback: Correct. _Edgington v Fitzmaurice_: a statement of intention is a representation as to the representor's present state of mind. If the intention is not genuinely held, it is a misrepresentation of existing fact.

options.text: Is a mere puff and not actionable
options.feedback: Statements in a formal prospectus go beyond puff. They are intended to be relied upon.

options.text: Only becomes a misrepresentation if the stated intention is not carried out
options.feedback: The question is whether the intention was genuinely held when stated, not whether it was later carried out.

oscoa_references: Edgington v Fitzmaurice (1885) 29 Ch D 459

question: A seller tells a buyer the business has good relationships with all its suppliers, which is true. Before the contract is signed, the main supplier terminates its contract. The seller does not inform the buyer. Applying _With v O'Flanagan_ [1936] Ch 575, is this a misrepresentation?

options.text: No — the statement was true when made
options.feedback: _With v O'Flanagan_ held there is a continuing duty to correct representations that become false before the contract is concluded.

options.text: Yes — where a representation true when made becomes false before the contract is made, there is a duty to correct it; failure to do so is a misrepresentation
options.feedback: Correct. _With v O'Flanagan_ [1936]: representations are continuing. If they become false, the representor must correct them. Silence after the change constitutes a misrepresentation.

options.text: No — English law imposes no general duty of disclosure in contract
options.feedback: While there is no general duty of disclosure, a representation creates a continuing duty to correct if it becomes false.

options.text: Yes — but only if the contract is for the sale of a business
options.feedback: The principle applies to all contracts, not just business sales. In _With v O'Flanagan_ the contract was for a medical practice.

oscoa_references: With v O'Flanagan [1936] Ch 575

question: The tort of deceit (fraudulent misrepresentation) requires proof the representor:

options.text: Was negligent in making the statement
options.feedback: Negligence is insufficient for the tort of deceit. Fraud requires a dishonest state of mind.

options.text: Knew the statement was false, had no belief in its truth, or was reckless as to whether it was true or false — honest belief is a defence even if unreasonable
options.feedback: Correct. _Derry v Peek_ (1889) 14 App Cas 337: fraud requires knowledge, absence of honest belief, or recklessness. Carelessness without dishonesty is not fraud.

options.text: Breached a fiduciary duty to the representee
options.feedback: Fraud does not require a fiduciary relationship. Any person making a false statement knowingly or recklessly may be liable.

options.text: Profited personally from the representation
options.feedback: Profit is not an element of the tort of deceit. The focus is the dishonest state of mind.

oscoa_references: Derry v Peek (1889) 14 App Cas 337

question: A seller honestly but carelessly tells a buyer a machine produces 5,000 units per hour. In fact it produces 2,000 — discoverable from the manual the seller did not check. The buyer enters the contract. Under MA 1967, s.2(1), what is the buyer's claim?

options.text: No claim — the seller honestly believed the statement was true
options.feedback: Section 2(1) does not require proof of fraud or lack of honest belief by the claimant.

options.text: The buyer has a claim — the burden shifts to the seller to prove reasonable grounds and honest belief up to the time of contract; failing to check the manual likely defeats this. Damages: tortious (fraud) measure
options.feedback: Correct. MA 1967, s.2(1): once a misrepresentation is proved, the burden shifts to the representor to show reasonable grounds and honest belief. _Royscot Trust v Rogerson_ [1991] held damages under s.2(1) are assessed on the tortious (fraud) measure.

options.text: The buyer must prove a special relationship under _Hedley Byrne v Heller_ [1964]
options.feedback: Section 2(1) does not require a special relationship. It applies to all pre-contractual misrepresentations.

options.text: The buyer has a claim only for rescission, not damages
options.feedback: Section 2(1) specifically provides a right to damages. The court has discretion to award damages in lieu of rescission under s.2(2).

oscoa_references: Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.2(1)
Royscot Trust Ltd v Rogerson [1991] 2 QB 297

question: A buyer seeks to rescind a contract for a car purchased two years ago, which was induced by an innocent misrepresentation about mileage. The car has since been in an accident. Applying _Salt v Stratstone Specialist Ltd_ [2015] EWCA Civ 745, can the buyer rescind?

options.text: No — rescission is barred because the car cannot be returned in exactly the same condition (restitutio in integrum impossible at law)
options.feedback: _Salt v Stratstone_ [2015] held that in equity, substantial restoration may suffice. Exact restoration is not always required.

options.text: Possibly — equity may allow rescission where substantial restoration is possible, and the court can make an allowance for deterioration; the bar of restitutio in integrum is less strict in equity
options.feedback: Correct. _Salt v Stratstone_ [2015]: equity may grant rescission where substantial restoration (with monetary allowance for depreciation) is possible. This is a loophole — the common law bar is stricter than equity. The court may also award damages in lieu under MA 1967, s.2(2).

options.text: No — lapse of time (two years) is an absolute bar
options.feedback: Lapse of time is a bar only if the claimant has affirmed the contract or delayed unreasonably after discovering the misrepresentation. Two years alone is not automatic.

options.text: Yes — rescission is always available for any misrepresentation
options.feedback: Rescission is subject to bars: affirmation, lapse of time, impossibility of restitutio in integrum, and third-party rights.

oscoa_references: Salt v Stratstone Specialist Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 745
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.2(2)

question: In _Doyle v Olby (Ironmongers) Ltd_ [1969] 2 QB 158, the Court of Appeal held that damages for fraudulent misrepresentation are assessed:

options.text: On the contractual measure (expectation loss)
options.feedback: Fraud damages are tortious, not contractual. The claimant is entitled to all direct loss flowing from the fraud.

options.text: On the tortious measure — all direct loss flowing from the fraudulent inducement, including consequential losses, not limited by foreseeability
options.feedback: Correct. _Doyle v Olby_ [1969]: fraud damages put the claimant in the position they would have been in if the fraud had not been committed, not the position if the representation had been true. All direct loss is recoverable, even if unforeseeable.

options.text: On the restitutionary measure
options.feedback: The restitutionary measure is different. Fraud damages are tortious — the claimant recovers all loss caused by the fraud.

options.text: Limited to losses foreseeable at the time of the representation
options.feedback: Foreseeability does not limit fraud damages. All direct loss is recoverable regardless of foreseeability.

oscoa_references: Doyle v Olby (Ironmongers) Ltd [1969] 2 QB 158

question: Which of the following are bars to rescission for misrepresentation? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Affirmation — the representee, with knowledge of the misrepresentation, continues with the contract
options.feedback: Correct. Affirmation of the contract after discovering the misrepresentation bars rescission.

options.text: Lapse of time — unreasonable delay after discovering the misrepresentation
options.feedback: Correct. Unreasonable delay after knowledge of the misrepresentation may be treated as affirmation.

options.text: Impossibility of restitutio in integrum — the parties cannot be restored to their pre-contractual positions
options.feedback: Correct. However, equity may allow rescission where substantial restoration is possible — _Salt v Stratstone_ [2015].

options.text: Third-party rights have intervened — a bona fide purchaser for value without notice has acquired rights
options.feedback: Correct. If a third party has acquired rights in the subject matter in good faith and without notice, rescission is barred.

options.text: The misrepresentation was negligent rather than fraudulent
options.feedback: The type of misrepresentation does not affect the availability of rescission, though it affects damages. Rescission is available for all types.

oscoa_references: Salt v Stratstone Specialist Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 745
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.2(2)

question: A sophisticated investor signs a complex derivatives contract containing an entire agreement and non-reliance clause. The investor later claims the bank made pre-contractual negligent misrepresentations about the product's risks. Applying _Springwell Navigation Corp v JP Morgan Chase Bank_ [2010] EWCA Civ 1221, what is the position?

options.text: The investor can claim — MA 1967, s.3 renders all non-reliance clauses void
options.feedback: Section 3 subjects such clauses to the reasonableness test, not voidness.

options.text: The non-reliance clause likely bars the claim — where a sophisticated party signs a document acknowledging no reliance, contractual estoppel prevents them from asserting reliance, provided the clause is reasonable under UCTA 1977
options.feedback: Correct. _Springwell v JP Morgan_ [2010]: a non-reliance clause in a sophisticated commercial context, signed after negotiation, creates effective contractual estoppel. However, this is a loophole — the clause must survive UCTA 1977 reasonableness, and fraud is always an exception (_SERE Holdings v Volkswagen_ [2014]).

options.text: The bank has a duty to ensure the investor understood the risks regardless of the clause
options.feedback: No general duty of care to advise exists unless the bank assumed advisory responsibilities. _Springwell_ found the bank was contracting as principal, not adviser.

options.text: The clause is ineffective because it purports to exclude liability for misrepresentation
options.feedback: Non-reliance clauses prevent the misrepresentation claim by estopping the representee from proving reliance, not by excluding liability for established misrepresentations.

oscoa_references: Springwell Navigation Corp v JP Morgan Chase Bank [2010] EWCA Civ 1221
SERE Holdings Ltd v Volkswagen Group UK Ltd [2014] EWHC 1551 (Comm)
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3

question: [Gate C3] Under SGA 1979, s.12(1), there is an implied condition that the seller has the right to sell. Breach of this condition:

options.text: Entitles the buyer to damages only, not to reject the goods
options.feedback: Breach of s.12 is fundamental — it goes to the root of the contract.

options.text: Is a total failure of consideration — the buyer can recover the entire price even if the goods have been used
options.feedback: Correct. _Rowland v Divall_ [1923]: breach of s.12 is a total failure of consideration. The buyer recovers the full price.

options.text: Requires the buyer to prove the seller knew of the defect in title
options.feedback: Section 12(1) is a strict condition. The seller's knowledge is irrelevant.

options.text: Only applies if the contract is in writing
options.feedback: Section 12(1) is implied into all contracts for the sale of goods, oral or written.

oscoa_references: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.12(1)
Rowland v Divall [1923] 2 KB 500

question: [Gate C3] Which statement about CRA 2015 and digital content is correct?

options.text: Digital content is not covered by CRA 2015 — only physical goods and services are
options.feedback: CRA 2015, ss.34-36 introduced statutory protection for digital content for the first time.

options.text: Digital content must be of satisfactory quality, fit for purpose, and as described; remedies include repair/replacement, price reduction, and compensation for damage to devices caused by defective content
options.feedback: Correct. CRA 2015, ss.34-36 imply quality standards. Sections 42-46 provide remedies including compensation for device damage.

options.text: Digital content has the same short-term right to reject (30 days) as physical goods
options.feedback: Digital content has distinct remedies. The short-term right to reject under s.22 applies to goods, not digital content.

options.text: Digital content providers may exclude all statutory implied terms in their terms and conditions
options.feedback: CRA 2015, s.47 prohibits exclusion of the implied terms in consumer contracts.

oscoa_references: Consumer Rights Act 2015, ss.34-36, 42-47

question: [Gate C3] SGA 1979, s.13 implies a condition that goods sold by description must correspond with the description. This applies:

options.text: Only where the buyer has not seen the goods before purchase
options.feedback: _Beale v Taylor_ [1967]: sale by description applies even where the buyer has inspected the goods.

options.text: To all sales where the buyer relies on the description, even if the buyer has seen the goods — reliance on the descriptive words is key
options.feedback: Correct. SGA 1979, s.13: sale by description applies where the buyer relies on the description. Inspection does not necessarily displace reliance.

options.text: Only to sales of unascertained goods
options.feedback: Section 13 applies to both specific and unascertained goods sold by description.

options.text: Only where the description is in writing
options.feedback: An oral description is also a sale by description under s.13.

oscoa_references: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.13
Beale v Taylor [1967] 1 WLR 1193

question: [Gate C3] Under UCTA 1977, Schedule 2, which factor is NOT listed for assessing reasonableness?

options.text: The relative bargaining positions of the parties
options.feedback: This is listed at Schedule 2(a).

options.text: Whether the customer received an inducement to agree to the term
options.feedback: This is listed at Schedule 2(b).

options.text: Whether the term was drafted by a qualified solicitor
options.feedback: Correct. The identity of the drafter's legal adviser is not a statutory factor. The relevant factor is whether the customer knew or ought to have known of the term.

options.text: Whether the customer knew or ought reasonably to have known of the existence and extent of the term
options.feedback: This is listed at Schedule 2(c).

oscoa_references: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.11
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, Schedule 2

question: [Gate C3] UCTA 1977, s.3 applies where one party deals on the other's written standard terms of business. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Section 3 applies only to consumer contracts
options.feedback: Section 3 applies where one party contracts on the other's standard terms, B2B or B2C.

options.text: The party relying on standard terms cannot exclude or restrict liability for breach unless the term satisfies the reasonableness test
options.feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977, s.3(2): where one party deals on written standard terms, any exclusion or restriction of liability for breach must be reasonable under s.11.

options.text: Standard terms are always void under s.3
options.feedback: Section 3 does not void standard terms. It subjects exclusion clauses within them to reasonableness.

options.text: Section 3 only applies if the contract value exceeds £100,000
options.feedback: There is no monetary threshold in s.3. It applies regardless of contract value.

oscoa_references: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.3

question: [Gate C3] Under CRA 2015, s.68, a term in a consumer contract is transparent if:

options.text: It is printed in a font size of at least 10pt
options.feedback: Font size alone is not determinative. The test is broader.

options.text: It is expressed in plain and intelligible language and, in the case of a written term, is legible
options.feedback: Correct. CRA 2015, s.68: a term must be in plain and intelligible language and legible. Transparency is essential for the core term exemption under s.64 and is assessed as part of good faith under s.62.

options.text: It is contained in a document headed Terms and Conditions
options.feedback: The heading of the document is not determinative. The language and presentation matter.

options.text: It is less than 500 words
options.feedback: Length is not the test. A short but complex term may be non-transparent. A long but clear term may be transparent.

oscoa_references: Consumer Rights Act 2015, s.68

question: [Gate C3] In _Prenn v Simmonds_ [1971] 1 WLR 1381, the House of Lords held that evidence of prior negotiations and the parties' subjective declarations of intent are:

options.text: Always admissible as part of the factual matrix
options.feedback: The House of Lords excluded such evidence. The factual matrix is limited to objective background.

options.text: Not admissible as an aid to contractual interpretation — the court looks at the objective meaning, not what the parties privately intended or said during negotiations
options.feedback: Correct. _Prenn v Simmonds_ [1971]: prior negotiations, draft agreements, and subjective declarations of intent are excluded from the factual matrix for interpretation.

options.text: Admissible but only if the contract is ambiguous
options.feedback: The exclusion in _Prenn v Simmonds_ is not conditional on ambiguity. Such evidence is generally inadmissible.

options.text: Admissible if the parties agree to admit them
options.feedback: The admissibility of evidence is a question of law, not party agreement. Negotiation history is excluded by the rule in _Prenn v Simmonds_.

oscoa_references: Prenn v Simmonds [1971] 1 WLR 1381

question: [Gate C3] In _Tillman v Egon Zehnder Ltd_ [2019] UKSC 32, the Supreme Court held that the blue pencil test for severance permits:

options.text: The court to rewrite the clause to make it reasonable
options.feedback: The court cannot rewrite. The blue pencil test only removes words.

options.text: Removal of words if the remaining text makes grammatical sense without further modification or rearrangement of the remaining words
options.feedback: Correct. _Tillman_ [2019]: words can be struck through if the remainder makes grammatical sense without rearrangement. The court cannot rewrite, but can remove.

options.text: The entire clause to be struck out and replaced with a reasonable alternative
options.feedback: Replacement is rewriting, which exceeds the blue pencil test. Only removal is permitted.

options.text: Severance of any term that is unenforceable, regardless of grammatical consequence
options.feedback: The blue pencil test requires that the remaining text makes grammatical sense — the court cannot create nonsense.

oscoa_references: Tillman v Egon Zehnder Ltd [2019] UKSC 32

question: [Gate C3] A seller tells a buyer: The house has been let to reliable tenants. The seller omits that the tenants have given notice to leave in one month. Applying _Dimmock v Hallett_ (1866), is this a misrepresentation?

options.text: No — the statement was literally true (the tenants were reliable)
options.feedback: A half-truth creating a false impression is a misrepresentation. Omission of material facts can render a literally true statement misleading.

options.text: Yes — a literally true statement that omits material facts creating a misleading impression is a half-truth, which constitutes an actionable misrepresentation
options.feedback: Correct. _Dimmock v Hallett_ (1866): a half-truth — a statement that is literally true but misleading by omission — is a misrepresentation.

options.text: No — there is no duty of disclosure in English contract law
options.feedback: While no general duty exists, positive statements that create a false impression by omission trigger a duty to complete the picture.

options.text: Yes — but only if the contract is for the sale of land
options.feedback: The half-truth principle applies to all types of contracts, not just land.

oscoa_references: Dimmock v Hallett (1866) LR 2 Ch App 21

question: [Gate C3] _Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd_ [1964] AC 465 established liability for negligent misstatement. The key requirement is:

options.text: The statement was made by a qualified professional
options.feedback: Professional qualification is not essential. The test is the existence of a special relationship.

options.text: A special relationship of proximity between the representor and representee, where the representor assumed responsibility and the representee reasonably relied
options.feedback: Correct. _Hedley Byrne v Heller_ [1964]: a duty of care in negligent misstatement arises from a special relationship — voluntary assumption of responsibility and reasonable reliance.

options.text: The statement was made in writing
options.feedback: Oral statements can found _Hedley Byrne_ liability. The form of communication is not determinative.

options.text: The representor was paid for making the statement
options.feedback: Payment is not required. In _Hedley Byrne_, the bank's reference was provided gratuitously. What matters is assumption of responsibility.

oscoa_references: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: [Gate C3] Under MA 1967, s.2(2), the court may award damages in lieu of rescission. This remedy:

options.text: Is available for fraudulent misrepresentation
options.feedback: Section 2(2) is not available for fraudulent misrepresentation. It applies only to non-fraudulent misrepresentation.

options.text: Is discretionary and available for non-fraudulent misrepresentation where the court considers it equitable, having regard to the nature of the misrepresentation and the loss that rescission would cause
options.feedback: Correct. MA 1967, s.2(2): the court has discretion to award damages instead of rescission for innocent or negligent misrepresentation, considering the nature of the misrepresentation, the loss caused if the contract were upheld, and the loss that rescission would cause to the representor.

options.text: Is available as of right whenever rescission is barred
options.feedback: It is discretionary (the court may award), not mandatory. The court must consider the equities.

options.text: Provides the same measure of damages as s.2(1)
options.feedback: Section 2(2) damages are typically lower than the tortious measure under s.2(1). The court has broader discretion.

oscoa_references: Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.2(2)

question: [Gate C3] Under CRA 2015, a consumer's remedies for non-conforming goods follow a tiered structure. The correct sequence is:

options.text: Price reduction, then repair, then rejection
options.feedback: The sequence is the reverse. Rejection comes first.

options.text: Short-term right to reject (30 days), then repair or replacement, then price reduction or final right to reject
options.feedback: Correct. CRA 2015, ss.19-24: tier 1 — short-term rejection (30 days); tier 2 — repair or replacement; tier 3 — price reduction or final right to reject.

options.text: Repair only — consumers cannot reject goods
options.feedback: The CRA 2015 provides a right to reject, both short-term (s.22) and final (s.24).

options.text: The consumer may choose any remedy at any time without restriction
options.feedback: The remedies are tiered. The consumer must follow the hierarchy.

oscoa_references: Consumer Rights Act 2015, ss.19-24

question: [Gate C3] In _Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking_ [1971], the contract was formed when the driver took the ticket from the machine. The key principle is:

options.text: Terms may be incorporated after the contract is formed if they are reasonable
options.feedback: Terms must be incorporated before or at the time of contracting, not after.

options.text: Where a contract is formed by an automatic machine, terms cannot be introduced after the contract is made — all terms must be brought to the customer's attention before or at the time of contracting
options.feedback: Correct. _Thornton_ [1971]: the contract forms at the moment the ticket is taken. Post-contractual notices are not incorporated.

options.text: Automatic machines cannot form contracts — a human operator is required
options.feedback: Contracts can be formed by machines. The question is when and on what terms.

options.text: The customer has 14 days to reject any terms incorporated after contract formation
options.feedback: There is no such right. Terms introduced after formation simply are not part of the contract.

oscoa_references: Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking Ltd [1971] 2 QB 163

question: [Gate C3] In _Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd_ [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611, an entire agreement clause was held to:

options.text: Exclude all liability for misrepresentation, including fraud
options.feedback: Fraud can never be excluded by an entire agreement clause — _SERE Holdings v Volkswagen_ [2014].

options.text: Prevent the implication of terms and exclude liability for pre-contractual representations, provided it is clearly worded and not vitiated by fraud
options.feedback: Correct. _Inntrepreneur v East Crown_ [2000]: an entire agreement clause can exclude representations and restrict implied terms if clearly drafted.

options.text: Be void as an unfair term under the Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts Regulations 1999
options.feedback: The case involved commercial parties, not consumers. The court upheld the clause.

options.text: Be ineffective because the party did not read the clause
options.feedback: The party signed the agreement. Under _L'Estrange v Graucob_, signature binds even without reading.

oscoa_references: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611

question: [Gate C3] In _Smith v Land & House Property Corp_ (1884) LR 28 Ch D 7, a seller described a tenant as a most desirable tenant. The tenant was in arrears. The court held:

options.text: It was a statement of opinion and not actionable
options.feedback: The court found it was a statement of fact, not mere opinion.

options.text: The statement was a representation of fact — where a representor implies they know facts justifying their opinion, the statement may be treated as one of fact
options.feedback: Correct. _Smith v Land & House_ (1884): a statement of opinion may imply that the representor knows facts that justify the opinion. Where no such facts exist, the statement is a misrepresentation of fact.

options.text: Statements of opinion are never actionable in misrepresentation
options.feedback: An opinion that is not genuinely held, or that implies knowledge of justifying facts, may be actionable.

options.text: The seller was liable only in negligence, not misrepresentation
options.feedback: The seller was liable for misrepresentation. The statement about the tenant's desirability was treated as one of fact.

oscoa_references: Smith v Land & House Property Corp (1884) LR 28 Ch D 7

question: [Gate C3] Under MA 1967, s.3, a clause excluding or restricting liability for misrepresentation is:

options.text: Void in all circumstances
options.feedback: Section 3 does not void such clauses. It subjects them to the reasonableness test.

options.text: Only effective if it satisfies the requirement of reasonableness as stated in UCTA 1977, s.11(1) — the burden is on the party relying on the clause to prove reasonableness
options.feedback: Correct. MA 1967, s.3: any term excluding or restricting liability for misrepresentation (or remedies) must satisfy the UCTA 1977 reasonableness test. The burden of proving reasonableness is on the person claiming the term satisfies it.

options.text: Only applies to consumer contracts
options.feedback: Section 3 applies to all contracts, B2B and B2C alike.

options.text: Is automatically effective if contained in a signed written contract
options.feedback: Signature incorporates the clause but does not satisfy the statutory reasonableness requirement. The clause must still be reasonable.

oscoa_references: Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.11(1)

question: [Gate C3] Under CRA 2015, s.31, a trader cannot exclude or restrict liability for the implied terms in ss.9-17 (quality, fitness, description etc.) in consumer contracts. This prohibition is:

options.text: Subject to the reasonableness test
options.feedback: Unlike UCTA 1977, CRA 2015 s.31 does not apply a reasonableness test. The prohibition is broader.

options.text: Absolute — a term excluding liability for the CRA 2015 implied terms in a consumer contract is not binding on the consumer
options.feedback: Correct. CRA 2015, s.31: liability for the statutory implied terms cannot be excluded or restricted in consumer contracts. Any such term is not binding.

options.text: Waivable if the consumer signs a specific acknowledgment
options.feedback: Section 31 does not permit waiver. Consumer consent does not override the statutory protection.

options.text: Only applies to contracts with a value exceeding £100
options.feedback: Section 31 applies to all consumer contracts regardless of value.

oscoa_references: Consumer Rights Act 2015, s.31

question: [Gate C3] For a misrepresentation to be actionable, the statement must have induced the representee to enter the contract. _Edgington v Fitzmaurice_ (1885) established that:

options.text: The misrepresentation must be the sole inducement for entering the contract
options.feedback: _Edgington v Fitzmaurice_ held the opposite — it need not be the sole inducement.

options.text: It is sufficient that the representation was a reason (not necessarily the only reason) for the representee entering the contract, provided the representee relied on it
options.feedback: Correct. _Edgington v Fitzmaurice_: the misrepresentation need not be the sole inducement. It is enough that it was one of the reasons and was actively present in the representee's mind.

options.text: The representee must prove they would not have entered the contract at all but for the misrepresentation
options.feedback: The but-for test is not required. It is sufficient if the representation was a material inducement.

options.text: The representee need not prove reliance — it is presumed
options.feedback: Reliance must be proved, but the representee need not prove it was the only factor.

oscoa_references: Edgington v Fitzmaurice (1885) 29 Ch D 459

question: [Gate C3] In B2B sale of goods contracts, SGA 1979 implied terms (ss.13-15) may be excluded. Under UCTA 1977, s.6(3), such exclusion is:

options.text: Prohibited absolutely — SGA implied terms cannot be excluded in any contract
options.feedback: In B2B contracts, exclusion is possible subject to reasonableness. Only in consumer contracts is exclusion prohibited.

options.text: Permitted only if the exclusion clause satisfies the reasonableness test under s.11 — this is a loophole in B2B sales where the buyer has sufficient bargaining power to negotiate
options.feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977, s.6(3): in non-consumer (B2B) contracts, SGA implied terms may be excluded if reasonable. This allows sellers to limit liability where the buyer is commercially sophisticated — a significant loophole.

options.text: Absolutely permitted — businesses can always exclude implied terms
options.feedback: The exclusion must be reasonable under UCTA 1977. It is not automatic.

options.text: Prohibited if the goods are defective
options.feedback: The test is reasonableness, not the nature of the defect. Even for defective goods, a reasonable exclusion clause may be effective in B2B contracts.

oscoa_references: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.6(3)
Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.55

question: [Gate C3] Before the Misrepresentation Act 1967, the only remedy for innocent misrepresentation was rescission. The Act introduced:

options.text: A right to damages for all types of misrepresentation on the tortious measure
options.feedback: MA 1967 created different damages provisions for different types of misrepresentation.

options.text: Section 2(2) — a discretionary power for the court to award damages in lieu of rescission for non-fraudulent misrepresentation
options.feedback: Correct. MA 1967, s.2(2): where the representee would otherwise be entitled to rescind for non-fraudulent misrepresentation, the court may award damages instead if equitable, considering the nature of the misrepresentation and the loss rescission would cause.

options.text: A right to punitive damages for fraudulent misrepresentation
options.feedback: MA 1967 did not introduce punitive damages. It created statutory damages under s.2(1) and s.2(2).

options.text: Abolition of rescission for all types of misrepresentation
options.feedback: Rescission remains available. The Act supplemented, not replaced, the remedy of rescission.

oscoa_references: Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.2(2)

question: In _Barton v Armstrong_ [1976] AC 104, Armstrong threatened to kill Barton unless Barton executed a deed. The Privy Council held:

options.text: The threat was not duress because Barton also had commercial reasons for signing
options.feedback: The Privy Council held that duress need not be the sole reason for entering the contract.

options.text: It is sufficient that the duress was a reason for entering the contract — the burden then shifts to the defendant to prove the duress did not contribute to the decision
options.feedback: Correct. _Barton v Armstrong_ [1976]: once the claimant proves illegitimate pressure was a reason for the contract, the burden shifts to the defendant to prove it made no contribution.

options.text: Duress to the person requires the threat to be carried out before it can vitiate a contract
options.feedback: A threat of violence is sufficient. The threat need not be executed.

options.text: Duress renders a contract void, not voidable
options.feedback: Duress renders a contract voidable, not void ab initio. The victim may affirm or rescind.

oscoa_references: Barton v Armstrong [1976] AC 104

question: In _Pao On v Lau Yiu Long_ [1980] AC 614, the Privy Council identified the elements of economic duress. Which statement best captures the test?

options.text: Any commercial pressure amounts to economic duress
options.feedback: Legitimate commercial pressure is not duress. The pressure must be illegitimate.

options.text: Illegitimate pressure that compelled the victim's will, leaving no practical alternative but to agree, and the victim protested and did not subsequently affirm
options.feedback: Correct. _Pao On v Lau Yiu Long_ [1980]: economic duress requires (1) illegitimate pressure, (2) compulsion of the will, (3) no practical alternative, (4) protest, and (5) no subsequent affirmation.

options.text: Economic duress requires proof of physical threats as well as financial pressure
options.feedback: Economic duress and duress to the person are distinct. Economic duress concerns illegitimate economic pressure, not physical threats.

options.text: Any breach of contract threat constitutes economic duress
options.feedback: A threat to breach a contract may be economic duress but is not automatically so. The pressure must be illegitimate and must compel the victim.

oscoa_references: Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980] AC 614

question: A contractor threatens to stop work unless the employer agrees to an additional £100,000 payment due to genuinely unforeseen ground conditions that make the work much more expensive. The employer agrees under protest, fearing project delay costs of £500,000. Applying _DSND Subsea Ltd v Petroleum Geo-Services ASA_ [2000] BLR 530, is this economic duress?

options.text: Yes — the contractor's threat to stop work was illegitimate pressure
options.feedback: _DSND Subsea_ distinguishes between legitimate renegotiation in good faith and illegitimate pressure.

options.text: No — a threat to stop work in response to genuinely unforeseen circumstances, made in good faith during renegotiation, may be legitimate commercial pressure rather than illegitimate economic duress
options.feedback: Correct. _DSND Subsea_ [2000]: legitimate commercial pressure (renegotiation for unforeseen difficulties) differs from illegitimate duress. The loophole lies in distinguishing legitimate renegotiation from extortionate threats.

options.text: Yes — any threat to breach a contract constitutes economic duress
options.feedback: Not every threatened breach is duress. Context and good faith matter — _DSND Subsea_.

options.text: Yes — the employer had no practical alternative
options.feedback: Lack of practical alternative is one element, but the pressure must also be illegitimate. Good faith renegotiation may be legitimate even under pressure.

oscoa_references: DSND Subsea Ltd v Petroleum Geo-Services ASA [2000] BLR 530
Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980] AC 614

question: In _Allcard v Skinner_ (1887) 36 Ch D 145, a woman gifted property to a religious order. The court presumed undue influence because:

options.text: The religious order used physical force
options.feedback: The case concerned presumed undue influence from a relationship of trust and confidence, not physical force.

options.text: The relationship of spiritual adviser and devotee falls within Class 2A — relationships where the law presumes undue influence automatically
options.feedback: Correct. _Allcard v Skinner_ (1887): certain relationships (parent/child, solicitor/client, doctor/patient, religious adviser) give rise to an automatic presumption of undue influence. The burden shifts to the dominant party to rebut.

options.text: The gift was made without independent legal advice
options.feedback: Lack of independent advice is relevant to rebutting the presumption, but the presumption itself arose from the nature of the relationship.

options.text: The value of the gift was unreasonably high
options.feedback: Value is relevant to whether the transaction calls for an explanation but does not create the Class 2A presumption.

oscoa_references: Allcard v Skinner (1887) 36 Ch D 145

question: An elderly parent gifts their house to a child who manages their affairs. There is no solicitor/client or similar formal relationship. Applying _Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Etridge (No 2)_ [2001] UKHL 44, can undue influence be presumed?

options.text: No — the parent-child relationship was not listed as a Class 2A relationship in _Etridge_
options.feedback: Parent-child is a Class 2A relationship for influence from parent to child. But the gift here was from parent to child — Class 2B may apply.

options.text: Yes — under Class 2B, the parent can prove a relationship of trust and confidence existed and the transaction calls for explanation, raising a presumption of undue influence
options.feedback: Correct. _Etridge_ [2001]: Class 2B applies where the claimant proves a relationship of trust and confidence existed de facto and the transaction calls for explanation. The burden then shifts to the dominant party.

options.text: No — presumption only arises if the dominant party is a professional
options.feedback: Class 2B does not require professional status. Any relationship of trust and confidence may qualify.

options.text: Yes — all gifts between family members are presumed to result from undue influence
options.feedback: There is no blanket presumption for all family gifts. The relationship must be one of trust and confidence, and the transaction must call for explanation.

oscoa_references: Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Etridge (No 2) [2001] UKHL 44

question: In _Barclays Bank plc v O'Brien_ [1994] 1 AC 180, a wife guaranteed her husband's business debts. The House of Lords held the bank was put on inquiry. This means:

options.text: The bank must investigate every surety transaction
options.feedback: The bank need not investigate every transaction but must take reasonable steps once on inquiry.

options.text: Where a wife stands as surety for her husband's business debts in a non-commercial context, the bank has constructive notice of potential undue influence and must take reasonable steps to ensure informed consent
options.feedback: Correct. _Barclays Bank v O'Brien_ [1994]: a creditor is put on inquiry where the relationship between surety and debtor is non-commercial and the transaction is not to the surety's financial advantage.

options.text: The bank is automatically liable for any undue influence exerted by the debtor on the surety
options.feedback: The bank is not automatically liable. It must take reasonable steps (the _Etridge_ protocol). If it does, it is protected.

options.text: Only applies where the surety is a wife guaranteeing her husband's debts
options.feedback: Post-_Etridge_ [2001], the doctrine applies to all non-commercial sureties, not just wives.

oscoa_references: Barclays Bank plc v O'Brien [1994] 1 AC 180

question: A bank requires a wife to see a solicitor before executing a guarantee for her husband's business. The solicitor is also the husband's solicitor. The wife signs after a 10-minute meeting. Applying _Royal Bank of Scotland v Chandra_ [2011] EWCA Civ 192, is the bank protected?

options.text: Yes — the bank arranged a meeting with a solicitor as required by the _Etridge_ protocol
options.feedback: _Chandra_ [2011]: the advice must be genuinely independent and sufficient. A joint solicitor and rushed meeting may be inadequate.

options.text: No — the independent advice must be genuinely independent and sufficient; a solicitor acting for both parties and a cursory meeting may fail to meet the _Etridge_ standard
options.feedback: Correct. _RBS v Chandra_ [2011]: the loophole is ensuring the advice is genuinely independent and substantive. The solicitor must properly explain the nature and effect of the transaction and the risks.

options.text: Yes — the _Etridge_ protocol only requires that the surety meet with any solicitor
options.feedback: The protocol requires independent advice from a solicitor who is not also acting for the debtor in the transaction.

options.text: No — the bank is strictly liable for any undue influence by the husband
options.feedback: The bank's liability is for constructive notice, not strict liability. Compliance with the _Etridge_ protocol provides protection.

oscoa_references: Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Chandra [2011] EWCA Civ 192
Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Etridge (No 2) [2001] UKHL 44

question: In _Bell v Lever Brothers Ltd_ [1932] AC 161, the House of Lords held that common mistake renders a contract void only if:

options.text: One party was at fault for the mistake
options.feedback: If one party is at fault, other doctrines (misrepresentation, unilateral mistake) may apply.

options.text: The mistake renders the subject matter essentially and radically different from what the parties believed — mere difference in quality is insufficient
options.feedback: Correct. _Bell v Lever Bros_ [1932]: common mistake requires the contract to be essentially and radically different. In _Great Peace Shipping v Tsavliris_ [2002], the Court of Appeal confirmed this narrow scope and abolished equity's broader jurisdiction from _Solle v Butcher_.

options.text: Both parties made the same mistake as to the quality of the subject matter
options.feedback: Mistake as to quality alone is insufficient — _Bell v Lever Bros_.

options.text: The mistake was induced by a fraudulent misrepresentation
options.feedback: Fraudulent misrepresentation is a separate claim. Common mistake operates independently.

oscoa_references: Bell v Lever Brothers Ltd [1932] AC 161
Great Peace Shipping Ltd v Tsavliris Salvage (International) Ltd [2002] EWCA Civ 1407

question: Under SGA 1979, s.6, a contract for the sale of specific goods is void if the goods have perished without the seller's knowledge at the time the contract was made. True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: Correct. SGA 1979, s.6 codifies res extincta: where specific goods have perished at the time of the contract and the seller was unaware, the contract is void.

options.text: False
options.feedback: The statement accurately reflects s.6. However, if the seller was aware, different doctrines may apply (fraud, misrepresentation).

oscoa_references: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.6

question: In _Lewis v Averay_ [1972] 1 QB 198, a rogue posed as a famous actor to buy a car with a fraudulent cheque. The rogue sold the car to an innocent third party. The Court of Appeal held:

options.text: The contract was void for unilateral mistake as to identity — the car must be returned to the original owner
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal distinguished face-to-face transactions. In such cases, the contract is voidable, not void.

options.text: The contract was voidable for fraudulent misrepresentation — but because it was not rescinded before the third party acquired the car, the third party obtained good title
options.feedback: Correct. _Lewis v Averay_ [1972]: in face-to-face transactions, there is a strong presumption the seller intended to contract with the person present. The contract is voidable (not void), and title passes to a bona fide purchaser before rescission.

options.text: The contract was void ab initio and the third party acquired no title
options.feedback: This is the _Cundy v Lindsay_ approach for non-face-to-face contracts. _Lewis v Averay_ distinguished face-to-face dealings.

options.text: The third party was liable for conversion of the car
options.feedback: The third party acquired good title because the contract was voidable and had not been rescinded at the time of sale.

oscoa_references: Lewis v Averay [1972] 1 QB 198

question: A rogue uses a stolen identity to obtain finance for a car from Shogun Finance. The written contract identifies the finance customer by the stolen name. The rogue sells the car. Applying _Shogun Finance Ltd v Hudson_ [2003] UKHL 62, is the contract void or voidable?

options.text: Voidable — the contract was face-to-face with the rogue
options.feedback: The 3:2 majority distinguished between written contracts and face-to-face transactions.

options.text: Void — the written contract identified a specific person by name; there was no contract with the rogue because the finance company never intended to contract with him; thus the rogue never acquired title and nemo dat applies
options.feedback: Correct. _Shogun Finance v Hudson_ [2003] (3:2 majority): where the contract is in writing identifying the counterparty by name, and the identity is stolen, the contract is void. This is a powerful loophole — a void contract means title never passes, so the owner recovers the goods even from innocent third parties.

options.text: Void — all cases of mistaken identity make the contract void
options.feedback: _Lewis v Averay_ [1972] held face-to-face contracts are voidable, not void. _Shogun_ was distinguished because it was a written contract.

options.text: The third party acquired good title under SGA 1979, s.23
options.feedback: Section 23 (sale under voidable title) did not apply because the contract was held void, not voidable.

oscoa_references: Shogun Finance Ltd v Hudson [2003] UKHL 62
Lewis v Averay [1972] 1 QB 198
Cundy v Lindsay (1878) 3 App Cas 459

question: In _Patel v Mirza_ [2016] UKSC 42, the Supreme Court abandoned the reliance test from _Tinsley v Milligan_ [1994] in favour of:

options.text: An automatic rule that illegality always defeats the claim
options.feedback: _Patel v Mirza_ rejected automatic rules in favour of a discretionary approach.

options.text: A range of factors approach: (a) the purpose of the prohibition, (b) any other relevant public policy, and (c) whether denying the claim would be proportionate
options.feedback: Correct. _Patel v Mirza_ [2016]: the court must consider the underlying purpose of the prohibition, any other public policy factors, and proportionality. This replaced the rigid reliance test from _Tinsley v Milligan_.

options.text: An absolute bar — no claim may succeed if it involves any element of illegality
options.feedback: The Supreme Court rejected an absolute bar. _Patel v Mirza_ introduced a flexible, factors-based approach.

options.text: A test that considers only whether the claimant must rely on the illegality to prove their claim
options.feedback: This was the _Tinsley v Milligan_ reliance test which _Patel v Mirza_ overruled.

oscoa_references: Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42
Tinsley v Milligan [1994] 1 AC 340

question: In _Nordenfelt v Maxim Nordenfelt Guns and Ammunition Co Ltd_ [1894] AC 535, the House of Lords held that a restraint of trade clause is enforceable only if:

options.text: It is reasonable with reference to the interests of the party imposing it
options.feedback: Reasonableness must be assessed with reference to both parties and the public interest.

options.text: It is reasonable both as between the parties and with reference to the public interest
options.feedback: Correct. _Nordenfelt_ [1894]: restraint of trade clauses are prima facie void. They are enforceable only if reasonable in the interests of both parties and the public.

options.text: The restrained party agreed to it voluntarily
options.feedback: Voluntary agreement is not sufficient. The clause must also be reasonable in scope and duration.

options.text: The restraint is limited to 12 months duration
options.feedback: There is no fixed time limit. The test is reasonableness in all the circumstances. In _Nordenfelt_, a worldwide and unlimited duration restraint was upheld in the sale of a business.

oscoa_references: Nordenfelt v Maxim Nordenfelt Guns and Ammunition Co Ltd [1894] AC 535

question: A non-compete clause states: The Employee shall not compete for 24 months in the UK. The court finds 24 months unreasonably long and the UK unreasonably wide. Under _Tillman v Egon Zehnder_ [2019], can the clause be saved by severance?

options.text: Yes — the court can strike out 24 and the UK
options.feedback: Removing both would leave ungrammatical text: For after termination in. The blue pencil test requires grammatical sense.

options.text: No — striking both would leave grammatically incomplete text. The loophole is to draft cascading clauses with tiered durations and geographies (24 months UK; if invalid, 12 months UK; if invalid, 12 months Greater London)
options.feedback: Correct. _Tillman_ [2019]: the blue pencil test allows striking words only if the remainder makes grammatical sense without rearrangement. Cascading clauses survive by providing fallback options.

options.text: Yes — the court can rewrite to 12 months and Greater London
options.feedback: The court cannot rewrite — only remove. _Tillman_ confirms this limitation.

options.text: No — the entire clause is void and cannot be severed under any circumstances
options.feedback: Severance is possible if the blue pencil test is satisfied. The problem here is that the test fails, not that severance is impossible.

oscoa_references: Tillman v Egon Zehnder Ltd [2019] UKSC 32

question: In _Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham Urban District Council_ [1956] AC 696, Lord Radcliffe defined frustration as occurring when:

options.text: Performance becomes merely more expensive or difficult
options.feedback: A mere increase in expense is not frustration — _Davis Contractors_.

options.text: A supervening event renders performance radically different from what the parties contemplated — not merely more onerous or expensive
options.feedback: Correct. _Davis Contractors_ [1956]: frustration requires a fundamental change in the nature of the contractual obligation, not mere hardship. The contract is not frustrated simply because performance becomes more costly.

options.text: One party is unable to perform for any reason
options.feedback: Inability to perform is not frustration unless caused by a supervening event that fundamentally changes the obligation.

options.text: The contract becomes unprofitable for one party
options.feedback: Unprofitability alone is not frustration. The doctrine does not exist to relieve parties from bad bargains.

oscoa_references: Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham Urban District Council [1956] AC 696

question: A person hires a room with a balcony to watch the King's coronation procession for £75. The King falls ill and the coronation is postponed. The hirer refuses to pay. Applying _Krell v Henry_ [1903] 2 KB 740, was the contract frustrated?

options.text: No — the room was still available and suitable for other uses
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal held the foundation of the contract was the coronation.

options.text: Yes — the coronation was the foundation of the contract; although performance was still possible (the room existed), it was radically different from what was contracted (viewing the coronation)
options.feedback: Correct. _Krell v Henry_ [1903]: the coronation was the foundation. Without it, the purpose was frustrated. Contrast _Herne Bay Steam Boat Co v Hutton_ [1903] where a boat hire for the naval review was not frustrated — the boat could still be used for other purposes.

options.text: No — commercial contracts cannot be frustrated
options.feedback: Frustration applies to all contracts. Commercial contracts can be frustrated, though the test is applied strictly.

options.text: Yes — all contracts dependent on an external event are frustrated if the event does not occur
options.feedback: Not all dependent contracts are frustrated. The event must be the sole foundation. If the contract has other concurrent purposes, it may survive.

oscoa_references: Krell v Henry [1903] 2 KB 740
Herne Bay Steam Boat Co v Hutton [1903] 2 KB 683

question: In _Maritime National Fish Ltd v Ocean Trawlers Ltd_ [1935] AC 524, a charterer had five trawlers but only three licences. The charterer allocated licences to its own trawlers and claimed the charter of the fifth (unlicensed) trawler was frustrated. The Privy Council held:

options.text: The contract was frustrated — the trawler could not legally operate without a licence
options.feedback: The Privy Council held the frustration was self-induced because the charterer chose not to allocate a licence.

options.text: The frustration was self-induced — the charterer had a choice in allocating licences and chose not to allocate one to the chartered vessel; a party cannot rely on frustration caused by their own election
options.feedback: Correct. _Maritime National Fish v Ocean Trawlers_ [1935]: self-induced frustration is not frustration. The charterer had the choice and chose not to licence the chartered vessel.

options.text: The contract was frustrated because the licensing decision was made by a third party (the government)
options.feedback: The government restricted the total number of licences, but the charterer chose how to allocate them.

options.text: The contract was frustrated but the charterer remained liable for the hire
options.feedback: If the contract were frustrated, both parties would be discharged. The Privy Council held it was not frustrated.

oscoa_references: Maritime National Fish Ltd v Ocean Trawlers Ltd [1935] AC 524

question: Under the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943, s.1(2), money paid before a frustrating event:

options.text: Is retained by the payee — loss lies where it falls
options.feedback: The Act provides for recovery, reversing the common law position established in _Chandler v Webster_ [1904].

options.text: Is recoverable by the payor, and money due but not yet paid ceases to be payable — subject to the court's power to allow the payee to retain expenses incurred
options.feedback: Correct. LR(FC)A 1943, s.1(2): prepaid money is recoverable; future obligations cease. The court may allow the payee to retain or recover a sum for expenses incurred in performing the contract.

options.text: Is recoverable in full without deduction for the payee's expenses
options.feedback: The court may allow the payee to retain a sum up to the amount of expenses incurred (s.1(2)).

options.text: Is governed exclusively by the terms of the contract, not the Act
options.feedback: The Act provides a default regime. However, parties may contract out of the Act's provisions.

oscoa_references: Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943, s.1(2)

question: A contract includes a force majeure clause covering flooding, earthquakes, and other natural disasters. A dam bursts upstream causing flooding. However, investigation reveals the dam would have burst even without the heavy rain because of pre-existing structural defects. Applying _Classic Maritime Inc v Limbungan Makmur SDN BHD_ [2019] EWCA Civ 1102, can the party rely on force majeure?

options.text: Yes — flooding occurred and is covered by the force majeure clause
options.feedback: _Classic Maritime_ requires but-for causation between the force majeure event and the failure to perform.

options.text: No — the party must prove it would have performed but for the force majeure event; if the dam would have burst anyway, the party was already unable to perform and force majeure does not apply
options.feedback: Correct. _Classic Maritime v Limbungan_ [2019]: force majeure requires but-for causation. If the party would have been unable to perform anyway, the force majeure clause does not assist. This is a critical loophole — the claimant must show the force majeure event caused the failure.

options.text: Yes — an event within the clause's definition triggers force majeure regardless of alternative causation
options.feedback: _Classic Maritime_ rejected this argument. Causation remains essential.

options.text: The clause is void for uncertainty under UCTA 1977
options.feedback: Force majeure clauses are not void for uncertainty. The issue is causation, not validity.

oscoa_references: Classic Maritime Inc v Limbungan Makmur SDN BHD [2019] EWCA Civ 1102

question: In _Hoenig v Isaacs_ [1952] 2 All ER 176, a decorator completed work on a flat for £750 but there were defects costing £55 to remedy. The Court of Appeal held:

options.text: The decorator was not entitled to any payment because performance was not entire
options.feedback: The court applied the doctrine of substantial performance — the contract was substantially performed despite minor defects.

options.text: The contract was substantially performed — the decorator was entitled to the contract price (£750) less a deduction for the cost of remedying defects (£55)
options.feedback: Correct. _Hoenig v Isaacs_ [1952]: substantial performance allows the contractor to recover the contract price less a deduction for defects. Contrast _Cutter v Powell_ (1795) where entire performance was required.

options.text: The decorator was entitled to the full £750 because the defects were trivial
options.feedback: The court deducted the cost of remedying defects. The doctrine of substantial performance does not mean full recovery regardless of defects.

options.text: The decorator was only entitled to a quantum meruit for benefit conferred
options.feedback: The court applied substantial performance and awarded the contract price with a deduction, not quantum meruit.

oscoa_references: Hoenig v Isaacs [1952] 2 All ER 176
Cutter v Powell (1795) 6 TR 320

question: In _Sumpter v Hedges_ [1898] 1 QB 673, a builder abandoned a project after completing work worth £333 of a £565 contract. The builder was unable to recover on the contract but could claim a ________ for the value of the benefit voluntarily accepted by the employer.
feedback: The correct answer is 'quantum meruit'. _Sumpter v Hedges_ [1898]: where a party voluntarily accepts the benefit of partial performance, the performing party can claim reasonable remuneration (quantum meruit) for the benefit conferred. However, where the other party has no choice but to accept, quantum meruit may not be available.
oscoa_references: Sumpter v Hedges [1898] 1 QB 673

question: In _Hochster v De La Tour_ (1853) 2 E & B 678, an employer told a courier one month before the start date his services were not needed. The courier sued immediately. The court held:

options.text: The courier had to wait until the contract start date to sue
options.feedback: The court established the doctrine of anticipatory breach — the innocent party may sue immediately.

options.text: The employer's clear repudiation before performance was due constituted an anticipatory breach, entitling the courier to sue immediately without waiting for the start date
options.feedback: Correct. _Hochster v De La Tour_ (1853): where one party clearly and unequivocally indicates they will not perform, the innocent party may treat this as a breach and sue immediately.

options.text: The courier had no claim — the contract had not commenced
options.feedback: The court rejected this argument. An anticipatory breach gives rise to a cause of action before the due date for performance.

options.text: The employer was entitled to withdraw without liability because no work had been done
options.feedback: The employer's repudiation was a breach of contract. The courier was entitled to damages immediately.

oscoa_references: Hochster v De La Tour (1853) 2 E & B 678

question: A contractor repudiates a contract. The employer refuses to accept the repudiation, continues performing its own obligations, and claims the full contract price. Applying _White & Carter (Councils) Ltd v McGregor_ [1962] AC 413, can the employer do this?

options.text: No — the employer must accept the repudiation and sue for damages
options.feedback: _White & Carter_ held the innocent party may elect to affirm the contract.

options.text: Yes — the innocent party may affirm the contract and claim the contract price, but subject to the limitation that the innocent party must have a legitimate interest in affirming rather than accepting the repudiation and claiming damages
options.feedback: Correct. _White & Carter v McGregor_ [1962]: the innocent party may affirm and claim the contract price. However, this is limited: affirmation is not available if the innocent party needs the other party's cooperation to perform, or if the innocent party has no legitimate interest in affirming.

options.text: Yes — the innocent party has an unfettered right to affirm
options.feedback: The right to affirm is not unfettered. Post-_White & Carter_, the legitimate interest limitation was developed (_The Alaskan Trader_ [1984]).

options.text: No — repudiation automatically terminates the contract
options.feedback: Repudiation does not automatically terminate the contract. The innocent party has an election to affirm or accept the repudiation.

oscoa_references: White & Carter (Councils) Ltd v McGregor [1962] AC 413

question: In _Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd_ [1962] 2 QB 26, the Court of Appeal classified the seaworthiness term as innominate (intermediate). Which statements about innominate terms are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Breach gives the right to terminate only if it deprives the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract
options.feedback: Correct. The test for whether breach of an innominate term is repudiatory is the seriousness of the consequences, not the nature of the term itself.

options.text: Innominate terms are classified based on the seriousness of the consequences of breach, not by pre-existing labels
options.feedback: Correct. _Hong Kong Fir_: the court looks at the effect of the breach, not whether the term is labelled a condition or warranty.

options.text: A term labelled a condition in the contract is always a condition
options.feedback: The label is not decisive — _Schuler AG v Wickman Machine Tool Sales Ltd_ [1974] AC 235.

options.text: Seaworthiness is always a condition going to the root of the contract
options.feedback: _Hong Kong Fir_ specifically held that seaworthiness could be breached in minor ways (e.g., insufficient crew for a few days) and was therefore innominate.

options.text: Damages are always available for breach of an innominate term regardless of whether the breach is repudiatory
options.feedback: Correct. Damages are always available for any breach. The right to terminate depends on the seriousness.

oscoa_references: Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd [1962] 2 QB 26
Schuler AG v Wickman Machine Tool Sales Ltd [1974] AC 235

question: An opera singer misses the first week of performances (out of a 12-week run). The theatre terminates the contract. Applying _Poussard v Spiers and Pond_ (1876) 1 QBD 410, was the theatre entitled to terminate?

options.text: No — the singer missed only a small fraction of the total performances
options.feedback: The court held the obligation to perform from the opening night was a condition.

options.text: Yes — the obligation to perform from the opening night was a condition going to the root of the contract; its breach entitled termination regardless of consequences
options.feedback: Correct. _Poussard v Spiers_ (1876): the obligation to perform from opening night was a condition. Contrast _Bettini v Gye_ (1876) where missing rehearsals was a warranty — only minor damages.

options.text: No — the theatre should have hired a replacement and claimed the cost difference as damages
options.feedback: The theatre was entitled to treat the breach as repudiatory because it went to the root of the contract.

options.text: Yes — any breach of a performance contract entitles termination
options.feedback: Not every breach of a performance contract is repudiatory. It depends on the classification of the term breached.

oscoa_references: Poussard v Spiers and Pond (1876) 1 QBD 410
Bettini v Gye (1876) 1 QBD 183

question: In _Robinson v Harman_ (1848) 1 Ex 850, Parke B stated the compensatory principle: damages for breach of contract should place the claimant:

options.text: In the position they were in before the contract was made
options.feedback: That is the reliance interest or tort measure, not the contractual measure.

options.text: In the same position as if the contract had been performed, so far as money can do it
options.feedback: Correct. _Robinson v Harman_ (1848): the expectation interest — the claimant is entitled to the benefit of the bargain. The aim is to put the claimant in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed.

options.text: In the position that prevents unjust enrichment of the defendant
options.feedback: That is the restitution interest (_AG v Blake_ [2001]), which is exceptional in contract.

options.text: In the same position as before the breach occurred
options.feedback: Damages are assessed by reference to the position had the contract been performed, not the position before breach.

oscoa_references: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850

question: In _Hadley v Baxendale_ (1854) 9 Ex 341, the court set out two limbs for recoverable damages. Limb 2 covers losses:

options.text: Arising naturally from the breach itself
options.feedback: That is limb 1. Limb 2 covers losses arising from special circumstances.

options.text: That may reasonably be supposed to have been in the contemplation of both parties at the time of contracting as the probable result of the breach, given special knowledge communicated by the claimant
options.feedback: Correct. _Hadley v Baxendale_: limb 2 covers losses arising from special circumstances that were communicated to the defendant at the time of contracting, and thus were within the parties' reasonable contemplation.

options.text: That are unforeseeable but factually caused by the breach
options.feedback: Unforeseeable losses are not recoverable under _Hadley v Baxendale_.

options.text: That were actually intended by the party in breach
options.feedback: The test is objective reasonable contemplation, not subjective intention.

oscoa_references: Hadley v Baxendale (1854) 9 Ex 341

question: A charterer returns a ship 9 days late. The owner loses a lucrative follow-on charter because the ship was unavailable. The charterer was not told about the follow-on charter. Applying _Transfield Shipping Inc v Mercator Shipping Inc (The Achilleas)_ [2008] UKHL 48, what damages can the owner recover?

options.text: The loss of the follow-on charter — it was a natural consequence of late return
options.feedback: The House of Lords limited recovery to the difference between market and charter rates for the overrun period.

options.text: The difference between the market rate and the charter rate for the 9-day overrun — the charterer did not assume responsibility for the loss of the follow-on charter
options.feedback: Correct. _The Achilleas_ [2008]: assumption of responsibility is an additional filter on _Hadley v Baxendale_. In shipping, convention caps liability at the market/charter rate differential. Loss of a follow-on charter is not within the charterer's assumed responsibility.

options.text: Nominal damages only — the loss was too remote
options.feedback: Damages were not nominal. The charterer was liable for the market rate differential for the overrun period.

options.text: Full expectation damages for the follow-on charter loss
options.feedback: The House of Lords rejected full expectation damages. The charterer had not assumed responsibility for this type of loss.

oscoa_references: Transfield Shipping Inc v Mercator Shipping Inc (The Achilleas) [2008] UKHL 48
Hadley v Baxendale (1854) 9 Ex 341

question: In _British Westinghouse Electric and Manufacturing Co v Underground Electric Railways Co of London_ [1912] AC 673, the House of Lords held that the duty to mitigate requires the claimant to:

options.text: Take every possible step regardless of cost to minimise loss
options.feedback: The duty is to take reasonable steps, not every possible step. Claimants need not incur unreasonable expense or risk.

options.text: Take reasonable steps to minimise loss — failure to do so bars recovery of losses that could have been avoided by reasonable action
options.feedback: Correct. _British Westinghouse_ [1912]: the claimant must take reasonable steps to mitigate. It is not a duty to the defendant but a limitation on recoverable damages.

options.text: Accept any offer from the defendant to settle the claim
options.feedback: The duty to mitigate does not require accepting settlement offers. Reasonable steps must be taken in the ordinary course.

options.text: Wait for the defendant's instructions before taking action
options.feedback: The claimant must take reasonable proactive steps. Waiting for instructions is not mitigating.

oscoa_references: British Westinghouse Electric and Manufacturing Co Ltd v Underground Electric Railways Co of London Ltd [1912] AC 673

question: [Gate C4] In _The Atlantic Baron_ [1979] QB 705, shipbuilders demanded a 10% price increase, threatening otherwise to breach. The buyer paid under protest but did not sue until 8 months later. The court held:

options.text: The buyer could recover the overpayment — the threat to breach was economic duress
options.feedback: The court found the buyer had affirmed the contract by waiting 8 months.

options.text: The buyer could not recover — although the pressure was illegitimate, the buyer affirmed the contract by delaying 8 months without protest or action
options.feedback: Correct. _The Atlantic Baron_ [1979]: even where illegitimate pressure exists, the victim must protest at the time and not subsequently affirm. Eight months of delay constituted affirmation.

options.text: The threat to breach a contract can never constitute economic duress
options.feedback: A threat to breach can be economic duress. But the victim must act promptly to avoid affirmation.

options.text: The price increase was voluntary — payment under protest is consent
options.feedback: Payment under protest is not consent. However, subsequent delay without further action may constitute affirmation.

oscoa_references: The Atlantic Baron [1979] QB 705

question: [Gate C4] In _The Universe Sentinel_ [1983] 1 AC 366, the House of Lords described the test for economic duress as whether the victim had:

options.text: No choice at all — duress requires the complete absence of consent
options.feedback: The test is no practical choice, not the complete absence of any choice.

options.text: No practical choice but to agree — their will was overborne by illegitimate pressure, and they protested at the time
options.feedback: Correct. _The Universe Sentinel_ [1983]: the victim must have no practical choice. The pressure must be illegitimate (not merely commercial), and the victim must protest.

options.text: A reasonable alternative available but at a higher cost
options.feedback: A higher-cost alternative may still be a practical alternative. The victim must have no practical alternative.

options.text: Signed the contract under protest but with legal advice
options.feedback: Legal advice is relevant but does not preclude economic duress. The key is no practical choice.

oscoa_references: The Universe Sentinel [1983] 1 AC 366

question: [Gate C4] The primary way to rebut the presumption of undue influence is:

options.text: Proof that the transaction was fair and reasonable
options.feedback: Fairness is relevant but is not the primary method of rebuttal. Independent legal advice is key.

options.text: Proof that the vulnerable party received independent legal advice about the nature and effect of the transaction before entering into it
options.feedback: Correct. _Inche Noriah v Shaik Allie Bin Omar_ [1929]: independent legal advice is the primary means of rebutting presumed undue influence. The advice must be competent and informed.

options.text: Proof that the dominant party acted in good faith
options.feedback: Good faith alone does not rebut the presumption. The presumption arises from the relationship irrespective of good faith.

options.text: Proof that the transaction was not disadvantageous to the vulnerable party
options.feedback: The absence of disadvantage may support rebuttal but is not sufficient on its own. Independent advice is the recognised method.

oscoa_references: Inche Noriah v Shaik Allie Bin Omar [1929] AC 127

question: [Gate C4] Under _Etridge (No 2)_ [2001], a bank is put on inquiry whenever a person stands as surety for another's debts in circumstances where:

options.text: The surety is married to the debtor
options.feedback: Post-_Etridge_, the doctrine is not limited to spouses. It applies more broadly.

options.text: The relationship between surety and debtor is non-commercial and the transaction is not to the surety's financial advantage
options.feedback: Correct. _Etridge_ [2001]: the bank is on inquiry in any non-commercial surety transaction. This includes spouses, cohabitants, parents, children, and other familial or non-commercial relationships.

options.text: The surety is an individual (not a company)
options.feedback: The inquiry doctrine applies to non-commercial relationships, not simply because the surety is an individual.

options.text: The surety has no income of their own
options.feedback: The surety's financial means are not the trigger. The test is the non-commercial nature of the relationship.

oscoa_references: Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Etridge (No 2) [2001] UKHL 44

question: [Gate C4] Before _Great Peace Shipping Ltd v Tsavliris_ [2002], equity had a broader jurisdiction to set aside contracts for common mistake than the common law. What did _Great Peace_ decide?

options.text: It affirmed equity's separate jurisdiction under _Solle v Butcher_ [1950]
options.feedback: _Great Peace_ specifically overruled _Solle v Butcher_.

options.text: It abolished equity's separate jurisdiction — there is no broader equitable doctrine of common mistake; the common law test in _Bell v Lever Bros_ governs
options.feedback: Correct. _Great Peace_ [2002]: _Solle v Butcher_ [1950] was overruled. There is no equitable jurisdiction to set aside contracts for common mistake on broader grounds. The common law test is the exclusive test.

options.text: It confirmed that common mistake renders a contract voidable, not void
options.feedback: Common mistake renders a contract void, not voidable. _Great Peace_ did not change this.

options.text: It held that mistake as to quality can render a contract void
options.feedback: _Great Peace_ confirmed _Bell v Lever Bros_: mere mistake as to quality is insufficient.

oscoa_references: Great Peace Shipping Ltd v Tsavliris Salvage (International) Ltd [2002] EWCA Civ 1407
Solle v Butcher [1950] 1 KB 671
Bell v Lever Brothers Ltd [1932] AC 161

question: [Gate C4] The plea of non est factum is available in principle to:

options.text: Anyone who signs a document without reading it
options.feedback: The plea is narrow. Carelessness bars it for most literate persons.

options.text: Those unable to read through blindness or illiteracy, or those tricked through no fault of their own into signing a document radically different from what they believed
options.feedback: Correct. _Saunders v Anglia_ [1971]: the plea is limited to the blind, illiterate, or those tricked without carelessness. The document must be radically different.

options.text: Only those under the age of 18
options.feedback: Non est factum has nothing to do with age. It concerns mistake as to the nature of the document.

options.text: Anyone who signs a document in a language they do not understand
options.feedback: While language barriers may be relevant, the plea requires radical difference and no carelessness. Signing a document without translation may constitute carelessness.

oscoa_references: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: [Gate C4] Under _Patel v Mirza_ [2016], the court may refuse to apply the illegality defence if doing so would be disproportionate. This is a loophole because:

options.text: It allows illegal contracts to always be enforced
options.feedback: The proportionality test does not always allow enforcement. It is a balancing exercise.

options.text: It permits a claimant tainted by illegality to recover where the purpose of the prohibition would not be enhanced by denying the claim, and denying the claim would be disproportionate to the illegality
options.feedback: Correct. _Patel v Mirza_ [2016]: the proportionality limb allows the court to consider whether denying the claim is a proportionate response. This creates a loophole — claimants can argue that denying recovery would be disproportionate to the illegality involved.

options.text: It overrules all statutory prohibitions
