options.feedback: _Patel v Mirza_ does not override statutes. It provides a framework for the common law defence of illegality.

options.text: Illegality is never a defence if the contract has been partly performed
options.feedback: Partial performance does not automatically defeat the illegality defence. The _Patel_ factors still apply.

oscoa_references: Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42

question: [Gate C4] In _Herbert Morris Ltd v Saxelby_ [1916] 1 AC 688, the House of Lords distinguished between restraints in:

options.text: Written and oral contracts
options.feedback: The distinction was not about the form of the contract.

options.text: Employment contracts (narrowly scrutinised) and sale of business contracts (more generously assessed)
options.feedback: Correct. _Herbert Morris v Saxelby_ [1916]: restraints in employment contracts are more strictly scrutinised because of the inequality of bargaining power. Sale of business restraints are more generously assessed (_Nordenfelt_ [1894]) because the vendor is selling goodwill and receives consideration.

options.text: Consumer and commercial contracts
options.feedback: The distinction in _Herbert Morris_ was between employment and sale of business contracts.

options.text: Contracts made before and after the Restraint of Trade Act 1969
options.feedback: There is no Restraint of Trade Act 1969. The common law governs.

oscoa_references: Herbert Morris Ltd v Saxelby [1916] 1 AC 688
Nordenfelt v Maxim Nordenfelt Guns and Ammunition Co Ltd [1894] AC 535

question: [Gate C4] _Taylor v Caldwell_ (1863) 3 B & S 826 is the foundational case on frustration. The music hall burned down before a concert series. The court held:

options.text: The hall owner must pay damages for non-performance
options.feedback: The court held the contract was frustrated. Neither party was in breach.

options.text: The contract was frustrated by the destruction of the subject matter — both parties were discharged from their obligations without fault
options.feedback: Correct. _Taylor v Caldwell_ (1863): the destruction of the hall rendered performance impossible. The contract was frustrated — the first clear articulation of the doctrine.

options.text: The contract was void for mistake
options.feedback: The hall existed at the time of contracting. Mistake concerns events existing at formation; frustration concerns supervening events.

options.text: The hall owner was liable in negligence for failing to prevent the fire
options.feedback: There was no allegation of negligence. The fire was accidental, and the contract was discharged by frustration.

oscoa_references: Taylor v Caldwell (1863) 3 B & S 826

question: [Gate C4] Frustration does not apply if the supervening event was foreseeable at the time of contracting. This principle is drawn from:

options.text: _Krell v Henry_ [1903]
options.feedback: _Krell v Henry_ concerned frustration of purpose. The foreseeability limitation is from _Davis Contractors_.

options.text: _Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC_ [1956] — where an event is foreseeable, the parties are presumed to have allocated the risk in the contract
options.feedback: Correct. _Davis Contractors_ [1956]: where an event was foreseeable, the contract is presumed to have allocated the risk. Frustration is reserved for truly unforeseen events.

options.text: _Taylor v Caldwell_ (1863)
options.feedback: _Taylor v Caldwell_ established the doctrine but did not discuss foreseeability.

options.text: _Maritime National Fish v Ocean Trawlers_ [1935]
options.feedback: _Maritime National Fish_ concerned self-induced frustration, not foreseeability.

oscoa_references: Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham Urban District Council [1956] AC 696

question: [Gate C4] In _The Mihalis Angelos_ [1971] 1 QB 164, the charterparty stated the vessel was expected ready to load on 1 July. The charterers cancelled on 17 July when the vessel was still not ready. The clause was held to be:

options.text: A warranty — the charterers could only claim damages for the delay
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal held the clause was a condition. The charterers were entitled to terminate.

options.text: A condition — the expected readiness clause was a condition; its breach entitled the charterers to terminate regardless of the consequences of the breach
options.feedback: Correct. _The Mihalis Angelos_ [1971]: the expected readiness clause was a condition. Breach entitled the charterers to terminate even if the delay was not substantial.

options.text: An innominate term — the right to terminate depended on the consequences of the breach
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal classified it as a condition, not an innominate term.

options.text: A representation, not a term of the contract
options.feedback: The clause was a term of the charterparty, not a mere representation.

oscoa_references: The Mihalis Angelos [1971] 1 QB 164

question: [Gate C4] In _Schuler AG v Wickman Machine Tool Sales Ltd_ [1974] AC 235, the contract labelled a term as a condition. The House of Lords held:

options.text: The label condition is decisive — the term is always a condition
options.feedback: The House of Lords held the label is not decisive. The court looks at the contract as a whole.

options.text: The label condition is not decisive — the court examines the whole contract and the consequences of breach to determine the true classification
options.feedback: Correct. _Schuler v Wickman_ [1974]: the label condition is a factor but not determinative. The court looks at the contract as a whole. This is a loophole — drafters cannot simply label terms as conditions to guarantee the right to terminate.

options.text: The term was void for uncertainty because it was mis-labelled
options.feedback: The term was not void. It was reclassified based on the contract's overall construction.

options.text: The label condition only matters in consumer contracts
options.feedback: The principle applies to all contracts. _Schuler_ was a commercial contract.

oscoa_references: Schuler AG v Wickman Machine Tool Sales Ltd [1974] AC 235

question: [Gate C4] In _Anglia Television Ltd v Reed_ [1972] 1 QB 60, an actor breached a contract to appear in a TV film. The court awarded wasted pre-production expenditure. This is the:

options.text: Expectation interest — what the claimant would have earned if the contract had been performed
options.feedback: The expectation interest would be the profit from the film, which was too speculative to calculate.

options.text: Reliance interest — putting the claimant in the position they would have been in if the contract had never been made, by recovering wasted expenditure
options.feedback: Correct. _Anglia Television v Reed_ [1972]: where the expectation interest is too speculative, the claimant may elect to claim the reliance interest — recovering expenditure wasted in reliance on the contract.

options.text: Restitution interest — recovering the defendant's profits
options.feedback: Restitution was not awarded. The aim was to compensate Anglia Television's wasted expenditure.

options.text: Nominal damages — a token award for the breach
options.feedback: The damages were substantial (wasted pre-production costs), not nominal.

oscoa_references: Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60

question: [Gate C4] In _Attorney General v Blake_ [2001] 1 AC 268, the House of Lords awarded an account of profits for breach of contract. This remedy is:

options.text: Routinely available for any breach of contract
options.feedback: _Blake_ is exceptional. The House of Lords stressed the remedy would only be available in extraordinary cases.

options.text: Exceptional — available only where the normal remedies (damages, specific performance, injunction) are inadequate and the claimant has a legitimate interest in preventing the defendant's profit-making activity
options.feedback: Correct. _AG v Blake_ [2001]: account of profits is exceptional. The claimant must have a legitimate interest in depriving the defendant of profit, and other remedies must be inadequate.

options.text: Never available for breach of contract — only for torts
options.feedback: _Blake_ confirmed the remedy is available for breach of contract in exceptional circumstances.

options.text: Available whenever the breach was deliberate
options.feedback: Deliberate breach alone does not justify an account of profits. The test in _Blake_ is more stringent.

oscoa_references: Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268

question: [Gate C4] In _Victoria Laundry (Windsor) Ltd v Newman Industries Ltd_ [1949] 2 KB 528, a boiler was delivered 5 months late. The laundry claimed lost profits from a particularly lucrative dyeing contract with the Ministry of Supply. The court held:

options.text: All lost profits were recoverable under limb 1 of _Hadley v Baxendale_
options.feedback: The special government contract was not a natural consequence of delay but depended on special circumstances.

options.text: Ordinary lost profits were recoverable under limb 1, but the particularly lucrative government contract was not recoverable under limb 2 because the defendant had no notice of it
options.feedback: Correct. _Victoria Laundry_ [1949]: ordinary lost profits (limb 1) are recoverable. Extraordinary profits from a special contract are only recoverable under limb 2 if the defendant had notice of the special circumstances at the time of contracting.

options.text: No lost profits were recoverable — loss of profit is always too remote
options.feedback: Lost profits are often recoverable under _Hadley v Baxendale_. The question is which lost profits.

options.text: All lost profits were recoverable under limb 2 because they were within reasonable contemplation
options.feedback: The defendant had no knowledge of the Ministry contract, so the loss was not within reasonable contemplation.

oscoa_references: Victoria Laundry (Windsor) Ltd v Newman Industries Ltd [1949] 2 KB 528
Hadley v Baxendale (1854) 9 Ex 341

question: [Gate C4] The reasonableness of steps taken to mitigate loss is assessed:

options.text: With the benefit of hindsight — what would have been the best course of action
options.feedback: Hindsight is not the standard. Claimants must act reasonably at the time.

options.text: At the time the decision was made — the claimant must act reasonably in the circumstances, not with the perfect vision of hindsight
options.feedback: Correct. _Banco de Portugal v Waterlow & Sons_ [1932]: the claimant is not required to do what, in hindsight, would have been the optimal course. They must act reasonably in the circumstances as they existed at the time.

options.text: By the defendant — the defendant may choose what steps the claimant must take
options.feedback: The defendant does not dictate mitigation steps. The standard is reasonable conduct by the claimant.

options.text: Subjectively — based on what the claimant believed was reasonable
options.feedback: The test is objective — what a reasonable person would do in the same circumstances.

oscoa_references: Banco de Portugal v Waterlow & Sons Ltd [1932] AC 452

question: [Gate C4] In _Hounga v Allen_ [2014] UKSC 47, the Supreme Court allowed an illegal immigrant to claim for discrimination despite her employment contract being tainted by illegality. This case demonstrates:

options.text: Illegality is never a defence to a statutory tort claim
options.feedback: The case did not establish a blanket rule. It was fact-specific.

options.text: The illegality defence does not automatically bar all claims — the court must consider the purpose of the prohibition and whether the claim is inextricably linked to the illegality
options.feedback: Correct. _Hounga v Allen_ [2014]: the illegality defence is fact-sensitive. The discrimination claim was not inextricably linked to the illegality of the employment contract. This aligns with the _Patel v Mirza_ approach.

options.text: Illegal immigrants have a general right to enforce employment contracts
options.feedback: The case did not establish that illegal immigrants can enforce all employment contracts. It was limited to the statutory discrimination claim.

options.text: The contract was valid despite the illegality
options.feedback: The contract was illegal. The question was whether the statutory tort claim could proceed despite the illegality of the contractual relationship.

oscoa_references: Hounga v Allen [2014] UKSC 47

question: [Gate C4] A Material Adverse Change (MAC) clause in an M&A agreement allows a buyer to withdraw if a MAC occurs. _Grupo Hotelero Urvasco SA v Carey Value Added SL_ [2013] established that MAC clauses are:

options.text: Interpreted broadly to protect the buyer from any adverse change
options.feedback: The court interpreted the clause narrowly. MAC clauses are strictly construed.

options.text: Interpreted narrowly — a MAC must be a change in the financial condition or business that is material and sustained, not a temporary downturn. This is a loophole: sellers argue the change is not sufficiently material or sustained
options.feedback: Correct. _Grupo Hotelero v Carey_ [2013]: MAC clauses are narrowly interpreted. The buyer must prove a material and sustained change. The loophole: sellers can argue the downturn is temporary or not material enough to trigger the clause.

options.text: Void as penalty clauses
options.feedback: MAC clauses are not penalties. They are conditions precedent to the buyer's obligation to complete.

options.text: Automatically triggered by any decline in revenue
options.feedback: A MAC requires a material and sustained change, not any decline. Temporary or minor changes do not trigger the clause.

oscoa_references: Grupo Hotelero Urvasco SA v Carey Value Added SL [2013] EWHC 1039 (Comm)

question: Specific performance is a discretionary equitable remedy. The primary requirement for its availability is:

options.text: The contract must be in writing
options.feedback: Writing is not a requirement for specific performance, though it may be for certain contracts (land).

options.text: Damages must be an inadequate remedy — typically satisfied where the subject matter is unique, such as land or rare goods
options.feedback: Correct. Specific performance is only available where damages are inadequate. This is typically satisfied for land contracts and unique chattels (SGA 1979, s.52).

options.text: The defendant must have acted fraudulently
options.feedback: Fraud is not a requirement. Specific performance is available for breach of contract regardless of fault.

options.text: The contract must have been performed for at least six months
options.feedback: Duration of performance is not a requirement. Specific performance is about what remains to be performed.

oscoa_references: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.52

question: A commercial lease requires the tenant to operate a supermarket continuously. The tenant closes the store. The landlord seeks specific performance of the obligation to trade. Applying _Co-operative Insurance Society Ltd v Argyll Stores (Holdings) Ltd_ [1998] AC 1, will the court grant specific performance?

options.text: Yes — the lease expressly required continuous trading
options.feedback: The House of Lords refused specific performance despite the express obligation.

options.text: No — a contract requiring constant supervision (running a business) is not specifically enforceable; the court will not supervise the day-to-day operation of a commercial enterprise
options.feedback: Correct. _Co-operative Insurance v Argyll Stores_ [1998]: specific performance will not be granted where it requires constant supervision by the court. Operating a business requires ongoing judicial oversight which the court cannot practicably provide.

options.text: Yes — damages are inadequate because closing the anchor tenant damages the entire shopping centre
options.feedback: While damages may be significant, the House of Lords held that the nature of the obligation (requiring constant supervision) was decisive.

options.text: No — specific performance is never available for commercial leases
options.feedback: Specific performance can be available for leases. The issue was the nature of the obligation — running a business — not the type of contract.

oscoa_references: Co-operative Insurance Society Ltd v Argyll Stores (Holdings) Ltd [1998] AC 1

question: In _American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd_ [1975] AC 396, the House of Lords set guidelines for granting interim injunctions. The first question is:

options.text: Has the claimant suffered irreparable harm?
options.feedback: Irreparable harm is part of the balance of convenience, not the first question.

options.text: Is there a serious question to be tried — the claim is not frivolous or vexatious
options.feedback: Correct. _American Cyanamid_ [1975]: the court first asks whether there is a serious question to be tried. If so, it considers adequacy of damages and the balance of convenience.

options.text: Has the defendant given an undertaking in damages?
options.feedback: The undertaking in damages is provided by the claimant, not the defendant.

options.text: Is the contract in writing?
options.feedback: The form of the contract is not a factor in the _American Cyanamid_ guidelines.

oscoa_references: American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd [1975] AC 396

question: A ________ injunction (formerly a Mareva injunction) prevents a defendant from dissipating assets before judgment, to ensure there are assets available to satisfy a judgment. Established in _Mareva Compania Naviera SA v International Bulkcarriers SA_ [1980] 1 All ER 213.
feedback: The correct answer is 'freezing'. _Mareva v International Bulkcarriers_ [1980] established that the court may grant a pre-judgment order freezing a defendant's assets to prevent dissipation. Now governed by CPR Part 25 and Practice Direction 25A.
oscoa_references: Mareva Compania Naviera SA v International Bulkcarriers SA [1980] 1 All ER 213

question: In _Cavendish Square Holding BV v El Makdessi_ [2015] UKSC 67, the Supreme Court reformulated the penalty doctrine. A clause is now a penalty if:

options.text: It is not a genuine pre-estimate of loss
options.feedback: This was the old _Dunlop_ test. _Makdessi_ replaced it with a broader test.

options.text: It imposes a detriment out of all proportion to any legitimate interest of the innocent party in the enforcement of the primary obligation
options.feedback: Correct. _Cavendish v Makdessi_ [2015]: a clause is penal only if it is extravagant, exorbitant, or unconscionable relative to the innocent party's legitimate interest. The genuine pre-estimate test from _Dunlop_ is subsumed within this broader test.

options.text: It is described as a penalty in the contract
options.feedback: The label does not determine whether the clause is a penalty. The court looks at substance, not form.

options.text: The sum payable exceeds the contract price
options.feedback: Exceeding the contract price may indicate a penalty but is not the test. The test is proportionality to the legitimate interest.

oscoa_references: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Talal El Makdessi [2015] UKSC 67
Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v New Garage and Motor Co Ltd [1915] AC 79

question: A service contract provides: The standard monthly fee is £500. If the customer terminates before 24 months, the fee for the entire period is recalculated at the non-discounted rate of £750 per month. Post-_Makdessi_ [2015], is this clause a penalty?

options.text: Yes — it imposes a higher charge upon early termination
options.feedback: The clause is structured as a primary price adjustment, not a secondary obligation triggered by breach.

options.text: No — it is a primary obligation (price adjustment), not a secondary obligation (liquidated damages for breach); the penalty doctrine only applies to secondary obligations
options.feedback: Correct. _Makdessi_ [2015]: the penalty doctrine applies only to secondary obligations (damages for breach), not primary obligations (price). This is a key loophole — structuring payment as a price adjustment rather than damages avoids the penalty rule.

options.text: Yes — the additional £250/month is a penalty because it exceeds the provider's legitimate interest
options.feedback: As a primary obligation, the penalty doctrine does not apply. The test for secondary obligations is proportionality to legitimate interest.

options.text: Yes — the increase from £500 to £750 is extravagant
options.feedback: The increase reflects the undiscounted rate. The clause is a price adjustment mechanism, not a penalty.

oscoa_references: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Talal El Makdessi [2015] UKSC 67
ParkingEye Ltd v Beavis [2015] UKSC 67

question: The doctrine of privity of contract, established in _Tweddle v Atkinson_ (1861) 1 B & S 393, provides that:

options.text: Third parties may enforce contracts made for their benefit
options.feedback: That is the opposite of the privity rule. Third parties generally cannot enforce.

options.text: Only parties to a contract can enforce it or have it enforced against them — a third party cannot enforce a contract even if it was made for their benefit
options.feedback: Correct. _Tweddle v Atkinson_ (1861): the common law doctrine of privity limits contractual rights and obligations to the contracting parties. This was modified by the C(RTP)A 1999.

options.text: Privity only applies to contracts under seal
options.feedback: Privity applies to all contracts, regardless of whether they are under seal (deeds).

options.text: Privity was abolished by the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999
options.feedback: The 1999 Act created an exception to privity, not its abolition. Privity remains the general rule.

oscoa_references: Tweddle v Atkinson (1861) 1 B & S 393
Beswick v Beswick [1968] AC 58

question: Under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, s.1, a third party may enforce a contract term if:

options.text: The third party is a close family member of one of the parties
options.feedback: Family relationship is not the test. The third party must be identified in the contract.

options.text: The contract expressly provides that the third party may enforce the term, or the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party and there is no contrary intention
options.feedback: Correct. C(RTP)A 1999, s.1: the third party may enforce if (a) the contract expressly so provides, or (b) the term purports to confer a benefit and on proper construction the parties did not intend the term to be unenforceable by the third party.

options.text: The third party has provided consideration
options.feedback: The Act was designed to overcome the need for third-party consideration. The third party need not provide consideration.

options.text: The contract is under seal
options.feedback: The 1999 Act applies to all contracts, not just deeds. The formality of the contract is irrelevant.

oscoa_references: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, s.1

question: A commercial contract between A and B confers a benefit on C, a company in the same group. The contract states: No term of this agreement shall be enforceable by any third party under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. Can C enforce the benefit?

options.text: Yes — C is expressly identified as a beneficiary and the 1999 Act overrides exclusion clauses
options.feedback: Section 1(4) of the 1999 Act expressly permits parties to exclude third-party enforcement. The Act does not override contractual exclusions.

options.text: No — under C(RTP)A 1999, s.1(4), the parties may expressly exclude third-party enforcement; the exclusion clause is effective and C has no right to enforce
options.feedback: Correct. The loophole is drafting an express exclusion of third-party rights under s.1(4) of the 1999 Act. Commercial parties routinely exclude the Act to prevent unintended third-party beneficiaries from enforcing contractual terms.

options.text: Yes — because the contract was made after the 1999 Act came into force
options.feedback: The Act applies to post-1999 contracts, but s.1(4) allows parties to contract out. The date of the contract is irrelevant where there is an express exclusion.

options.text: Yes — the exclusion clause is void under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
options.feedback: UCTA 1977 does not apply to clauses excluding the operation of the 1999 Act. The Act specifically permits exclusion.

oscoa_references: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, s.1(4)

question: A severance clause provides that if any part of the contract is found invalid, the remainder continues in force. In _Tillman v Egon Zehnder_ [2019] UKSC 32, the Supreme Court confirmed that the blue pencil test:

options.text: Allows the court to rewrite invalid terms
options.feedback: The court cannot rewrite. The blue pencil test only removes words.

options.text: Permits the court to strike out words from a clause if the remaining text makes grammatical sense without rearrangement
options.feedback: Correct. _Tillman_ [2019]: the blue pencil test allows deletion of words, not rewriting. The remainder must make grammatical sense without rearrangement.

options.text: Applies only to restraint of trade clauses
options.feedback: The blue pencil test applies to all contractual terms, not just restraints of trade.

options.text: Was abolished by _Tillman_
options.feedback: _Tillman_ confirmed and clarified the blue pencil test, not abolished it.

oscoa_references: Tillman v Egon Zehnder Ltd [2019] UKSC 32

question: An entire agreement clause that does not contain a carve-out for fraud will still exclude liability for fraudulent misrepresentation because it states the agreement constitutes the entire agreement. True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: Fraud vitiates all contracts. An entire agreement clause cannot exclude liability for fraud without clear and explicit language.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. _SERE Holdings v Volkswagen_ [2014]: fraud vitiates an entire agreement clause. The loophole for the party relying on the clause is to include an express carve-out: nothing in this clause shall exclude liability for fraud or fraudulent misrepresentation. Conversely, the loophole for the claimant is that fraud always carves out entire agreement clauses even without express language.

oscoa_references: SERE Holdings Ltd v Volkswagen Group UK Ltd [2014] EWHC 1551 (Comm)
Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611

question: Under the Limitation Act 1980, s.5, the limitation period for a simple contract claim is:

options.text: 3 years from the date of knowledge
options.feedback: 3 years from knowledge applies to personal injury (s.11) and latent damage (s.14A) in tort, not simple contract claims.

options.text: 6 years from the date the cause of action accrued (the date of breach)
options.feedback: Correct. Limitation Act 1980, s.5: 6 years from the date of breach. For deeds, the period is 12 years under s.8.

options.text: 12 years from the date of breach
options.feedback: 12 years is the limitation period for deeds under s.8, not simple contracts.

options.text: 6 years from the date the claimant discovered the breach
options.feedback: For contract claims, time runs from the date of breach, not discovery. Discovery-based limitation applies in specific circumstances (fraud, concealment, mistake — s.32).

oscoa_references: Limitation Act 1980, s.5

question: A building contractor deliberately conceals defective foundation work by covering it with concrete in 2015. The defect causes cracking discovered by the client in 2024. The contractor argues the claim is time-barred under LA 1980, s.5 (6 years from breach in 2015). The client invokes s.32. What is the position?

options.text: The claim is time-barred — 6 years from 2015 expired in 2021
options.feedback: Section 32 postpones the limitation period where there has been deliberate concealment.

options.text: The claim may not be time-barred — under s.32, where any fact relevant to the claimant's right of action has been deliberately concealed by the defendant, time runs from the date the claimant discovered the concealment or could with reasonable diligence have discovered it
options.feedback: Correct. LA 1980, s.32: deliberate concealment postpones the limitation period until discovery. This is a powerful loophole — arguing that the defendant knowingly concealed the breach extends or revives time-barred claims. _Sheldon v RHM Outhwaite_ [1996].

options.text: The claim is automatically time-barred for all construction contracts
options.feedback: There is no automatic time bar for construction contracts. The standard limitation rules apply, subject to s.32.

options.text: The claim is subject to a 15-year longstop under s.14B
options.feedback: Section 14B applies to negligence claims, not contract claims. The longstop for contract claims subject to s.32 is not expressly limited by a longstop.

oscoa_references: Limitation Act 1980, s.32
Sheldon v RHM Outhwaite (Underwriting Agencies) Ltd [1996] AC 102

question: A written contract for freight forwarding contains an entire agreement clause. Before signing, the freight forwarder orally promised the customer: Your containers will always be carried below deck. The written contract allows above-deck carriage. The container is carried above deck and damaged by seawater. Applying _J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd_ [1976] 1 WLR 1078, can the customer claim?

options.text: No — the entire agreement clause excludes all prior oral representations
options.feedback: _Andrea Merzario_ held that an oral promise can constitute a collateral contract operating alongside the written agreement.

options.text: Yes — the oral promise may constitute a collateral contract separate from the main agreement; the entire agreement clause does not automatically exclude collateral contracts
options.feedback: Correct. _Andrea Merzario_ [1976]: a collateral oral warranty can circumvent an entire agreement clause. This is a classic loophole — arguing the pre-contractual statement is a separate collateral contract.

options.text: No — the customer signed the contract and is bound by the written terms under _L'Estrange v Graucob_
options.feedback: While signature binds to the main contract, a separate collateral contract exists independently. _L'Estrange_ does not apply to the collateral contract.

options.text: Yes — but only under the Misrepresentation Act 1967
options.feedback: The oral promise was a contractual term (collateral contract), not merely a representation. The claim was in contract, not misrepresentation.

oscoa_references: J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078
City of Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129

question: A client wants liquidated damages of £500,000 for early termination of a 5-year IT services contract. Estimated losses from early termination are approximately £300,000. Post-_Makdessi_, the best drafting approach is:

options.text: Liquidated damages clause: Upon early termination, the Customer shall pay £500,000 as liquidated damages
options.feedback: The sum exceeds the legitimate interest and may be challenged as a penalty under _Makdessi_.

options.text: Price adjustment mechanism: The Contract Price is based on a 5-year term. In the event of early termination, the Price shall be recalculated as if the contract had a shorter minimum term, with the monthly fee adjusted upward to reflect unamortised setup costs, transition costs, and lost volume discounts
options.feedback: Correct. Post-_Makdessi_, the safest approach is to structure the payment as a primary obligation (price adjustment) rather than a secondary obligation (damages). The drafting must identify and document the legitimate commercial interest protected by the clause.

options.text: No clause — rely on common law damages
options.feedback: Common law damages provide a fallback but do not give the contractual certainty the client seeks.

options.text: Penalty clause described honestly as a penalty — the clause will be reformed by the court
options.feedback: Deliberately drafting a penalty is ineffective. The court will not enforce a penalty even if it is honestly described as one.

oscoa_references: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Talal El Makdessi [2015] UKSC 67
ParkingEye Ltd v Beavis [2015] UKSC 67

question: In _Classic Maritime Inc v Limbungan Makmur SDN BHD_ [2019] EWCA Civ 1102, the Court of Appeal held that a party claiming force majeure must prove ________ causation — that it would have performed but for the force majeure event.
feedback: The correct answer is 'but-for'. _Classic Maritime v Limbungan_ [2019]: the claimant must show that but for the force majeure event, it would have performed. If the party was already unable or unwilling to perform, the force majeure clause does not apply. This is a critical loophole — force majeure is not an escape from pre-existing non-performance.
oscoa_references: Classic Maritime Inc v Limbungan Makmur SDN BHD [2019] EWCA Civ 1102

question: In _Yam Seng Pte Ltd v International Trade Corporation Ltd_ [2013] EWHC 111 (QB), Leggatt J found an implied duty of good faith in a relational contract. Which factor was key?

options.text: The contract was in the financial services sector
options.feedback: The contract was a distribution agreement. The sector was not the key factor.

options.text: The contract was a long-term relational contract requiring mutual cooperation, trust, and confidence — these characteristics justified implying good faith
options.feedback: Correct. _Yam Seng_ [2013]: relational contracts (long-term, mutual cooperation, high trust) may imply a duty of good faith. This is a loophole — English law traditionally rejects good faith. _Bates v Post Office_ [2019] and _Al Nehayan v Kent_ [2018] extended this.

options.text: The contract expressly provided for good faith
options.feedback: The contract was silent on good faith. The term was implied, not express.

options.text: The contract was governed by the laws of Singapore
options.feedback: The contract was governed by English law. The implication of good faith was under English common law.

oscoa_references: Yam Seng Pte Ltd v International Trade Corporation Ltd [2013] EWHC 111 (QB)
Bates v Post Office Ltd (No 3) [2019] EWHC 606 (QB)
Al Nehayan v Kent [2018] EWHC 333 (Comm)

question: A buyer wants exclusive negotiation rights for 6 months to buy a competitor, but does not want the negotiations to create a binding sale contract. Best drafting approach:

options.text: Single document headed Subject to Contract covering both exclusivity and sale terms
options.feedback: Subject to contract creates a presumption against binding effect for the entire document, including the exclusivity provision.

options.text: Two separate documents: (1) a legally binding Lock-Out Agreement with a fixed 6-month term (enforceable post-_Walford v Miles_), and (2) all substantive sale negotiations conducted Subject to Contract
options.feedback: Correct. This is the _Walford v Miles_ [1992] loophole: a fixed-term lock-out agreement is enforceable, while substantive negotiations remain non-binding subject to contract. Letters of intent must expressly state no binding obligations exist (_RTS Flexible Systems_ [2010]).

options.text: An agreement to negotiate in good faith for a reasonable period
options.feedback: An agreement to negotiate in good faith is unenforceable for uncertainty — _Walford v Miles_ [1992]. The period must be fixed.

options.text: A memorandum of understanding covering all commercial terms
options.feedback: An MOU covering all terms may be treated as a binding contract if sufficiently certain. The safer approach is separate documents as described.

oscoa_references: Walford v Miles [1992] 2 AC 128
RTS Flexible Systems Ltd v Molkerei Alois Muller GmbH & Co KG (UK Production) [2010] UKSC 14

question: An employer wants a non-compete clause that will survive blue-pencil scrutiny. Applying _Tillman v Egon Zehnder_ [2019] UKSC 32, the best drafting approach is:

options.text: A single broad clause: shall not compete anywhere in the UK for 24 months
options.feedback: A single broad clause cannot be severed if any part is unreasonable — the blue pencil test requires grammatical coherence.

options.text: Cascading tiered restrictions: (a) 24 months UK-wide, or if invalid (b) 12 months UK-wide, or if invalid (c) 12 months in Greater London, or if invalid (d) 6 months in Greater London. The court enforces the first valid tier
options.feedback: Correct. _Tillman_ [2019]: cascading clauses provide fallbacks. Each tier must make grammatical sense independently. If tier (a) is invalid, the court proceeds to (b), and so on. This is the post-_Tillman_ severance loophole.

options.text: A clause that asks the court to amend the clause to whatever is reasonable
options.feedback: The court cannot rewrite. A request for the court to amend is ineffective.

options.text: No non-compete clause — they are always void
options.feedback: Non-compete clauses are not always void. They are enforceable if reasonable in scope, duration, and geography (_Nordenfelt_ [1894]).

oscoa_references: Tillman v Egon Zehnder Ltd [2019] UKSC 32
Nordenfelt v Maxim Nordenfelt Guns and Ammunition Co Ltd [1894] AC 535

question: A creditor is owed £50,000. The debtor can only pay £40,000. The creditor agrees to accept £40,000 in full settlement. How can the creditor make this binding while avoiding the rule in _Foakes v Beer_ (1884)?

options.text: A simple oral agreement accepting the reduced payment
options.feedback: A simple agreement lacks consideration and is void under _Foakes v Beer_. The creditor could still sue for the remaining £10,000.

options.text: Execute a deed varying the contract — a deed does not require consideration under LP(MP)A 1989, s.1, and thus bypasses _Foakes v Beer_
options.feedback: Correct. A deed is the classic _Foakes v Beer_ loophole. Because deeds do not require consideration, the variation is binding. The deed must be in writing, expressed to be a deed, validly executed, and delivered.

options.text: Send a letter confirming the agreement — the postal rule makes it binding
options.feedback: The postal rule governs acceptance, not consideration. The agreement still lacks consideration for the creditor's promise to accept less.

options.text: Rely on promissory estoppel — the debtor cannot recover the £10,000 difference
options.feedback: Promissory estoppel is a shield, not a sword (_Combe v Combe_ [1951]), and _Re Selectmove_ [1995] held it cannot circumvent _Foakes v Beer_ for debt.

oscoa_references: Foakes v Beer (1884) 9 App Cas 605
Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989, s.1
Combe v Combe [1951] 2 KB 215
Re Selectmove Ltd [1995] 1 WLR 474

question: A party enters a contract illegal under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 but is unaware of the illegality. The other party, who knew of the illegality, refuses to perform after receiving a £100,000 deposit. Under _Patel v Mirza_ [2016], can the innocent party recover the deposit?

options.text: No — illegality always defeats a claim, regardless of fault
options.feedback: _Patel v Mirza_ replaced the automatic illegality bar with a factors-based test.

options.text: Potentially yes — applying the _Patel v Mirza_ factors: the purpose of FSMA is to regulate financial services, not prevent restitution; denying recovery may be disproportionate where the claimant was unaware of the illegality and the defendant knowingly participated
options.feedback: Correct. _Patel v Mirza_ [2016]: the proportionality limb. Where the purpose of the prohibition would not be enhanced by denying the claim and denial would be disproportionate, recovery may be allowed. This is a significant loophole post-_Patel_.

options.text: Yes — but only if the contract was performed for more than one year
options.feedback: Duration of performance is not a factor in the _Patel v Mirza_ analysis.

options.text: No — the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 creates a statutory bar that overrides the _Patel_ approach
options.feedback: Statutory illegality is subject to the same _Patel v Mirza_ analysis, unless the statute expressly provides otherwise.

oscoa_references: Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42

question: A loan agreement provides: The Lender may bring proceedings in any court of competent jurisdiction. The Borrower may only bring proceedings in the courts of England. This asymmetric jurisdiction clause:

options.text: Is void — jurisdiction clauses must be mutual
options.feedback: Asymmetric clauses are generally valid in English law, though they are scrutinised in some EU member states.

options.text: Is valid in English law — asymmetric clauses are generally enforceable, giving the Lender maximum flexibility while restricting the Borrower. The loophole: draft asymmetric clauses for a tactical advantage in forum selection
options.feedback: Correct. Asymmetric jurisdiction clauses are valid in English law (_Unicredit Bank AG v Euronav NV_ [2022] EWHC 957). They give one party forum-shopping flexibility while restricting the other. The counter-tactic is to negotiate a mutual exclusive jurisdiction clause.

options.text: Is void under the Arbitration Act 1996
options.feedback: The Arbitration Act 1996 does not govern jurisdiction clauses, which are about court proceedings, not arbitration.

options.text: Is only valid if both parties are domiciled in the UK
options.feedback: Domicile is not a prerequisite. Asymmetric clauses are valid regardless of the parties' domicile, subject to applicable jurisdiction rules.

oscoa_references: Unicredit Bank AG v Euronav NV [2022] EWHC 957 (Comm)

question: A contract contains a NOM clause. The parties enter an oral variation. They perform in accordance with the oral variation for 3 years before a dispute arises. Applying _MWB v Rock Advertising_ [2018] UKSC 24, can the oral variation be effective?

options.text: No — the NOM clause invalidates all oral variations regardless of conduct
options.feedback: _MWB v Rock Advertising_ held that NOM clauses can be waived by mutual conduct.

options.text: Yes — both parties may waive the NOM clause by their words or conduct; 3 years of performance in accordance with the oral variation evidences mutual waiver
options.feedback: Correct. _MWB v Rock Advertising_ [2018]: NOM clauses are effective, but the parties may mutually waive them. Three years of performance consistent with the oral variation is strong evidence of waiver. This is the NOM clause loophole — waiver by conduct.

options.text: No — the NOM clause can only be waived by a written agreement
options.feedback: _MWB_ held that NOM clauses can be waived orally or by conduct. The clause itself may be subject to the same informal variation it purports to prohibit.

options.text: Yes — oral variations always override written terms
options.feedback: _MWB_ explicitly rejected the position that oral variations always override NOM clauses. The variation is invalid unless the NOM clause is waived.

oscoa_references: MWB Business Exchange Centres Ltd v Rock Advertising Ltd [2018] UKSC 24

question: [Gate C5] Specific performance will not be granted for contracts of personal service because:

options.text: Personal service contracts lack consideration
options.feedback: Employment contracts have consideration. The bar on specific performance is policy-based, not consideration-based.

options.text: It would compel involuntary servitude and require constant supervision of the employment relationship
options.feedback: Correct. _Warner Bros v Nelson_ [1937]: courts will not compel an individual to work for another. However, negative covenants (not to work for competitors) may be enforced by injunction.

options.text: Personal service contracts are void ab initio
options.feedback: Personal service contracts are valid. The limitation is on the remedy, not the contract.

options.text: Damages are always an adequate remedy in personal service cases
options.feedback: Damages are often inadequate, especially for unique talent. The bar is policy-based, not based on adequacy of damages.

oscoa_references: Warner Bros Pictures Inc v Nelson [1937] 1 KB 209

question: [Gate C5] An Anton Piller order (now a search order under CPR 25.1(1)(h)) is:

options.text: An order freezing the defendant's bank accounts
options.feedback: Freezing bank accounts is a freezing injunction (formerly Mareva), not a search order.

options.text: A mandatory order requiring the defendant to permit the claimant to enter premises to search for, preserve, and take copies of evidence that might otherwise be destroyed
options.feedback: Correct. _Anton Piller KG v Manufacturing Processes_ [1976]: a search order permits the claimant to enter premises to secure evidence at risk of destruction. Now governed by CPR 25 and Practice Direction 25A.

options.text: An order requiring the defendant to disclose the location of assets worldwide
options.feedback: That is a disclosure order ancillary to a freezing injunction, not a search order.

options.text: An order for specific performance of a contractual obligation
options.feedback: A search order is not related to contract performance. It is an evidence-preservation remedy.

oscoa_references: Anton Piller KG v Manufacturing Processes Ltd [1976] Ch 55

question: [Gate C5] In _ParkingEye Ltd v Beavis_ [2015] UKSC 67, a £85 parking charge for overstaying was upheld. The Supreme Court held the charge was not a penalty because:

options.text: £85 was a genuine pre-estimate of ParkingEye's loss
options.feedback: The charge was not a genuine pre-estimate. ParkingEye's actual loss from overstaying was negligible.

options.text: ParkingEye had a legitimate interest in deterring overstaying to ensure turnover of parking spaces for the benefit of the retail stores and the public — the charge was not extravagant or unconscionable with reference to that interest
options.feedback: Correct. _ParkingEye v Beavis_ [2015]: the test is whether the clause is proportionate to a legitimate interest. Deterrence of overstaying to maintain parking availability was a legitimate interest, and £85 was not extravagant.

options.text: The charge was a primary obligation and the penalty doctrine did not apply
options.feedback: The charge was a secondary obligation (triggered by breach — overstaying), but was upheld under the _Makdessi_ legitimate interest test.

options.text: The motorist agreed to the charge in the terms and conditions
options.feedback: Consent alone does not save a penalty. The charge had to be assessed under the _Makdessi_ test despite consent.

oscoa_references: ParkingEye Ltd v Beavis [2015] UKSC 67

question: [Gate C5] In _Beswick v Beswick_ [1968] AC 58, a nephew agreed to pay an annuity to his uncle, and after the uncle's death to the uncle's widow. The nephew stopped paying. The widow sued as administratrix and personally. The House of Lords held:

options.text: The widow could enforce the contract as a third-party beneficiary
options.feedback: As a third party, the widow could not enforce under the privity doctrine. The C(RTP)A 1999 had not yet been enacted.

options.text: The widow could enforce in her capacity as administratrix (suing on behalf of the deceased uncle's estate) and was granted specific performance — but she could not enforce in her personal capacity as a third party
options.feedback: Correct. _Beswick v Beswick_ [1968]: the widow as administratrix stood in the uncle's shoes (a contracting party) and could enforce. As a third party in her personal capacity, she could not. The C(RTP)A 1999 now allows third-party enforcement.

options.text: The widow had no claim — only contracting parties may enforce a contract
options.feedback: The widow succeeded as administratrix of the uncle's estate. She was suing in a representative capacity.

options.text: The contract was void for lack of consideration by the widow
options.feedback: The uncle had provided consideration. The issue was privity, not consideration.

oscoa_references: Beswick v Beswick [1968] AC 58

question: [Gate C5] Under C(RTP)A 1999, s.1(3), a third party must be expressly identified in the contract by:

options.text: Full legal name and registered address
options.feedback: The Act does not require full name and address. Identification by class or description is sufficient.

options.text: Name, as a member of a class, or as answering a particular description — the third party need not be in existence at the time of the contract
options.feedback: Correct. C(RTP)A 1999, s.1(3): identification by name, class membership, or particular description suffices. The third party need not exist at the time of contracting (e.g., future subsidiaries).

options.text: Date of birth and national insurance number
options.feedback: The Act imposes no such requirements. The test is can the third party be identified from the contract?

options.text: By name only — general descriptions are insufficient
options.feedback: Section 1(3) expressly permits identification by class or particular description, not just by name.

oscoa_references: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, s.1(3)

question: [Gate C5] A contract contains a NOM clause. The parties agree orally to vary the payment date, and the payer pays on the new date. The payee accepts. Later the payee argues the oral variation is invalid under the NOM clause. Under _MWB v Rock Advertising_ [2018], what is the position?

options.text: The oral variation is invalid — the NOM clause is conclusive
options.feedback: _MWB_ held that NOM clauses can be waived. Conduct consistent with the variation may evidence waiver.

options.text: The parties may have waived the NOM clause by their conduct — acceptance of payment on the new date is evidence of mutual waiver
options.feedback: Correct. _MWB v Rock Advertising_ [2018]: NOM clauses are effective but may be waived. The loophole is that parties can waive NOM clauses by conduct, so payment and acceptance behaviour may override the written clause.

options.text: The oral variation is valid — all contracts can always be varied orally
options.feedback: _MWB_ rejected the common law rule that oral variations always override NOM clauses.

options.text: The NOM clause is void as an unfair term under CRA 2015
options.feedback: NOM clauses are not per se unfair. And in a commercial contract, CRA 2015 does not apply.

oscoa_references: MWB Business Exchange Centres Ltd v Rock Advertising Ltd [2018] UKSC 24

question: [Gate C5] The Limitation Act 1980, s.8 provides a limitation period for actions on a specialty (deed) of:

options.text: 6 years from the date of breach
options.feedback: 6 years is the period for simple contracts under s.5. Deeds have a longer period.

options.text: 12 years from the date the cause of action accrued
options.feedback: Correct. LA 1980, s.8: 12 years for deeds, compared to 6 years for simple contracts under s.5. This is another advantage of using deeds.

options.text: 3 years from the date of knowledge
options.feedback: 3 years from knowledge applies to personal injury and latent damage (tort), not deeds.

options.text: No limitation period — deeds are enforceable indefinitely
options.feedback: Even deeds are subject to limitation. The period is 12 years under s.8, not indefinite.

oscoa_references: Limitation Act 1980, s.8

question: [Gate C5] In _Springwell Navigation Corp v JP Morgan Chase Bank_ [2010] EWCA Civ 1221, the Court of Appeal upheld a non-reliance clause preventing a misrepresentation claim. The key factor was:

options.text: The clause was in bold capital letters
options.feedback: The presentation of the clause was relevant to incorporation but not the decisive factor.

options.text: The contract was between sophisticated commercial parties, negotiated at arm's length, and the non-reliance clause was reasonable under UCTA 1977 — creating contractual estoppel
options.feedback: Correct. _Springwell_ [2010]: in a sophisticated B2B context with negotiated terms, non-reliance clauses create effective contractual estoppel. The loophole: commercial parties can draft strong non-reliance clauses that bar misrepresentation claims, provided they are reasonable.

options.text: The investor was a consumer and the clause was fair under CRA 2015
options.feedback: Springwell was a sophisticated corporate entity, not a consumer. CRA 2015 was not applicable.

options.text: The bank had a fiduciary duty to the investor
options.feedback: The court found no fiduciary relationship. The bank was dealing as principal, not as an adviser.

oscoa_references: Springwell Navigation Corp v JP Morgan Chase Bank [2010] EWCA Civ 1221

question: [Gate C5] To prevent a court from implying a duty of good faith into a long-term commercial contract, the best drafting approach is:

options.text: Silence — good faith is never implied in English law
options.feedback: _Yam Seng_ [2013] and _Bates v Post Office_ [2019] have shown good faith may be implied in relational contracts. Silence is risky.

options.text: Express disclaimer: No duty of good faith is to be implied into this agreement and each party's obligations are strictly as set out in this agreement
options.feedback: Correct. Given _Yam Seng_, _Bates_, and _Al Nehayan_, the counter-tactic is an express exclusion of any implied duty of good faith. This prevents the relational contract argument.

options.text: Include a governing law clause choosing New York law
options.feedback: New York law also implies good faith in certain contexts. A change of law is not a reliable solution.

options.text: Limit the contract term to less than one year
options.feedback: Short-term contracts can still be relational if they involve mutual cooperation and trust. Duration is one factor, not determinative.

oscoa_references: Yam Seng Pte Ltd v International Trade Corporation Ltd [2013] EWHC 111 (QB)
Bates v Post Office Ltd (No 3) [2019] EWHC 606 (QB)

question: [Gate C5] A contract prohibits assignment of contractual rights. A party assigns its rights without consent. Under _Linden Gardens Trust Ltd v Lenesta Sludge Disposals Ltd_ [1994] 1 AC 85, the assignment is:

options.text: Valid — contractual prohibitions on assignment are void
options.feedback: The House of Lords held that contractual prohibitions on assignment are effective.

options.text: Ineffective against the other contracting party — the prohibition is effective; the assignment cannot transfer the right to sue. The loophole: draft with consent not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed
options.feedback: Correct. _Linden Gardens v Lenesta Sludge_ [1994]: prohibitions on assignment are effective. Breach renders the assignment ineffective. The loophole is to draft permissive assignment clauses with the qualification that consent shall not be unreasonably withheld or delayed.

options.text: Valid — the assignee can enforce the right directly
options.feedback: The House of Lords held the prohibition prevented the assignment from being effective.

options.text: The prohibition is effective only if it refers to the specific right being assigned
options.feedback: A general prohibition on assignment is effective if clearly worded. Specific reference to particular rights is not required.

oscoa_references: Linden Gardens Trust Ltd v Lenesta Sludge Disposals Ltd [1994] 1 AC 85

question: [Gate C5] Under the Arbitration Act 1996, s.69, an appeal on a point of law from an arbitral award:

options.text: Is available as of right to any dissatisfied party
options.feedback: Section 69 requires leave of the court. It is not available as of right.

options.text: Requires leave of the court, is available only if the question is one which the tribunal was asked to determine, and the decision is obviously wrong or of general public importance — the loophole is that parties may contract out of s.69 entirely
options.feedback: Correct. Arbitration Act 1996, s.69: limited appeal rights requiring leave. The loophole: parties can exclude s.69 in the arbitration agreement, making the award final and binding with no appeal on points of law.

options.text: Is available for any error of fact or law in the award
options.feedback: Section 69 is limited to points of law, not errors of fact. The court does not review factual findings.

options.text: Must be brought within 14 days of the award
options.feedback: The time limit under s.70(3) is 28 days from the date of the award, not 14 days.

oscoa_references: Arbitration Act 1996, s.69

question: [Gate C5] Before _Makdessi_ [2015], the classic test for a penalty from _Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co v New Garage_ [1915] was:

options.text: Whether the clause was described as a penalty in the contract
options.feedback: The label is not determinative. The court looks at substance.

options.text: Whether the sum stipulated was a genuine pre-estimate of loss (liquidated damages) or a sum extravagant and unconscionable in amount intended to deter breach (penalty)
options.feedback: Correct. _Dunlop v New Garage_ [1915]: the test was whether the sum was a genuine pre-estimate of loss. _Makdessi_ [2015] broadened this to the legitimate interest test.

options.text: Whether the contract was in writing
options.feedback: The form of the contract is irrelevant to the penalty doctrine.

options.text: Whether the penalty clause was in a consumer contract
options.feedback: The penalty doctrine applies to all contracts, not just consumer contracts.

oscoa_references: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v New Garage and Motor Co Ltd [1915] AC 79
Cavendish Square Holding BV v Talal El Makdessi [2015] UKSC 67

question: [Gate C5] Material Adverse Change (MAC) clauses in M&A agreements are interpreted:

options.text: Broadly to protect the buyer from any adverse financial event
options.feedback: Courts interpret MAC clauses narrowly against the party seeking to invoke them.

options.text: Narrowly — the change must be material, sustained, and not merely a temporary downturn; the burden is on the buyer to prove the MAC. The loophole for sellers is to argue the change is neither material nor sustained
options.feedback: Correct. _Grupo Hotelero v Carey_ [2013]: MAC clauses are narrowly interpreted. The buyer bears the burden of proving a material and sustained adverse change.

options.text: As void for uncertainty — they are never enforceable
options.feedback: MAC clauses are enforceable but narrowly interpreted. They are not void for uncertainty.

options.text: Under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 reasonableness test
options.feedback: UCTA 1977 does not regulate MAC clauses. The issue is interpretation, not statutory control.

oscoa_references: Grupo Hotelero Urvasco SA v Carey Value Added SL [2013] EWHC 1039 (Comm)

question: [Gate C5] Limitation Act 1980, s.14A provides an alternative limitation period for latent damage claims of 3 years from date of knowledge, subject to a longstop of:

options.text: 6 years
options.feedback: 6 years is the standard contract period. The longstop for latent damage is longer.

options.text: 15 years from the date of the negligent act or omission
options.feedback: Correct. LA 1980, s.14A/14B: for latent damage in negligence (not contract), 3 years from date of knowledge, subject to a 15-year longstop from the date of the negligent act.

options.text: 12 years
options.feedback: 12 years is the limitation period for deeds under s.8, not the latent damage longstop.

options.text: There is no longstop — claims can be brought at any time after discovery
options.feedback: Section 14B imposes a 15-year longstop. Claims are barred after 15 years regardless of when the damage was discovered.

oscoa_references: Limitation Act 1980, s.14A
Limitation Act 1980, s.14B

question: [Gate C5] _Dwyer (UK Franchising) Ltd v Fredbar Ltd_ [2021] EWHC 1218 suggests the best approach for post-termination non-compete clauses in franchise agreements is:

options.text: A single broad clause with the maximum duration the franchisor wants
options.feedback: A single broad clause is vulnerable to being struck down if unreasonable.

options.text: Cascading step-down clauses with progressive reductions in duration and scope — if one tier is unreasonable, the court moves to the next
options.feedback: Correct. _Dwyer v Fredbar_ [2021]: tiered/cascading non-compete clauses survive scrutiny by providing fallback options. The court enforces the first tier that is reasonable.

options.text: No post-termination restriction — they are always void in franchise agreements
options.feedback: Post-termination restrictions in franchise agreements can be enforceable if reasonable.

options.text: An indemnity clause instead of a non-compete clause
options.feedback: An indemnity serves a different purpose (compensation) and does not replace a non-compete clause (restraint).

oscoa_references: Dwyer (UK Franchising) Ltd v Fredbar Ltd [2021] EWHC 1218
Tillman v Egon Zehnder Ltd [2019] UKSC 32

question: [Gate C5] Under _Williams v Roffey Bros_ [1991] 1 QB 1, a promise to pay extra for performance of an existing contractual duty may be supported by consideration if:

options.text: The promise is made in writing
options.feedback: Writing is not a requirement. The test is practical benefit.

options.text: The promisor obtains a practical benefit (e.g., avoiding a penalty clause, avoiding the need to find a replacement)
options.feedback: Correct. _Williams v Roffey Bros_ [1991]: practical benefit suffices as consideration. This is a loophole — it softens the rule in _Stilk v Myrick_. However, _Foakes v Beer_ (part-payment of debt) remains good law under _MWB v Rock Advertising_ [2018].

options.text: The promisee threatened to breach if not paid extra
options.feedback: A threat to breach may constitute economic duress, not consideration. _Williams v Roffey_ involved a request for extra payment, not a threat.

options.text: The contract is under seal
options.feedback: Practical benefit under _Williams v Roffey_ applies to all contracts, not just deeds.

oscoa_references: Williams v Roffey Bros & Nicholls (Contractors) Ltd [1991] 1 QB 1
MWB Business Exchange Centres Ltd v Rock Advertising Ltd [2018] UKSC 24

question: [Gate C5] Limitation Act 1980, s.32 postpones limitation where the defendant has deliberately concealed a fact relevant to the claimant's right of action. Deliberate concealment requires:

options.text: Active steps to conceal — mere silence or failure to disclose is insufficient
options.feedback: Section 32(2) deems deliberate commission of a breach itself to conceal the breach.

options.text: Either active steps to conceal, or deliberate commission of a breach which is unlikely to be discovered for some time — s.32(2) deems such a breach to involve deliberate concealment
options.feedback: Correct. LA 1980, s.32: deliberate concealment includes active concealment and, under s.32(2), deliberate commission of a breach unlikely to be discovered. This is a powerful loophole — it extends time for deliberate wrongs even without active cover-up.

options.text: Written acknowledgment by the defendant that they concealed the facts
options.feedback: No written acknowledgment is required. The test is evidence of deliberate concealment or deliberate breach.

options.text: Proof that the concealment was fraudulent
options.feedback: Under s.32(2), deliberate breach is sufficient without proving dishonest concealment beyond the breach itself. _Sheldon v RHM Outhwaite_ [1996].

oscoa_references: Limitation Act 1980, s.32
Sheldon v RHM Outhwaite (Underwriting Agencies) Ltd [1996] AC 102

question: [Gate C5] A key drafting point for force majeure clauses post-_Classic Maritime v Limbungan_ [2019] is:

options.text: The clause should be as brief as possible to avoid interpretation disputes
options.feedback: Brevity leads to gaps. Comprehensive drafting is safer.

options.text: Include a non-exhaustive list of force majeure events, specify causation requirements (materially hinder rather than but-for), and address notice obligations and mitigation
options.feedback: Correct. Post-_Classic Maritime_ [2019], drafters should specify the causation standard (e.g., materially hinder may be easier to prove than but-for) and define events comprehensively. Include notice time bars and mitigation duties.

options.text: Force majeure clauses are void under UCTA 1977 and should be avoided
options.feedback: Force majeure clauses are not void under UCTA 1977. They are standard commercial clauses that allocate risk.

options.text: The clause should only cover acts of God
options.feedback: Acts of God alone are too narrow. Comprehensive force majeure clauses should cover a broad range of events.

oscoa_references: Classic Maritime Inc v Limbungan Makmur SDN BHD [2019] EWCA Civ 1102

question: [Gate C5] A letter of intent can create binding obligations despite being labelled non-binding. To avoid this, the best drafting approach is:

options.text: Avoid all letters of intent — they are always binding
options.feedback: Letters of intent are not always binding. Careful drafting can avoid unintended contractual obligations.

options.text: Expressly state the letter does not create a binding contract, specify that any work done will be paid on a quantum meruit basis only, and cap liability at a stated amount
options.feedback: Correct. _RTS Flexible Systems_ [2010]: clear wording prevents binding obligations. Specify that work is at the supplier's risk, to be paid quantum meruit (reasonable value) only, with a liability cap.

options.text: Use the phrase subject to contract — this always prevents a binding agreement
options.feedback: Subject to contract can be waived by conduct — _RTS Flexible Systems_ [2010]. Additional protections (quantum meruit, liability cap) are recommended.

options.text: Sign the letter of intent as a deed
options.feedback: Executing as a deed would make the letter binding, defeating the purpose of a non-binding letter of intent.

oscoa_references: RTS Flexible Systems Ltd v Molkerei Alois Muller GmbH & Co KG (UK Production) [2010] UKSC 14
